A small probe bet (~250 into 700, ~35% pot) will fold out a lot of Elon’s missed or pot‑control hands and only commits a small amount if I’m called. Plan: bet 250 as a bluff, fold to a check‑raise or any large raise, and if called I’ll give up on most turn blanks and only continue with a 5 (made straight) or very favorable action.

Facing a small 250 bet into 950 pot (1/4 pot) on a dry 2c-6s-3h rainbow flop that completely whiffs my JQo. No pair, no straight draw (JQ would need T), no flush draw potential realized. Preflop as SB raiser after button limp, my range is perceived strong, but check-fold is standard OOP with air here. Minni's limp-call range is wide (pairs 22-AA, suited connectors like 45/34/56, Ax, broadways), and this board hits her speculative hands frequently (small pairs set-mine value, gutshots). Her small bet could be thin value, probe, or weak float, but calling commits me to multi-street OOP with ~20% pot odds requiring showdown value or equity I lack. Raising as a bluff risks her having a real hand and OOP dynamics worsen postflop. Deep stacks (10700 effective), better to fold cleanly and wait for equity spots. Long-term EV maximized by check-fold air on uncoordinated boards.

Ts6h is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and dominated by better tens or sixes. On the button with deep stacks, position allows a wide range, but this hand lacks strong potential for straights or draws and performs poorly post-flop against likely calling ranges from blinds. Opponents show aggression in history, but folding avoids tough spots OOP for them or multiway pots. Better to preserve stack for premium opportunities over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack suited (Ah Jh), which is a premium hand in a short-handed game. Since Elon (the Button) has folded, it is between me and Minni (the Big Blind). I want to take control of the pot and build value. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here to either win the pot immediately or set up a favorable post-flop situation with a strong range advantage.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind with Td6s in the big blind. This is a marginal, offsuit hand and not a good 3-bet candidate — especially out of position — but the open is only ~3bb and my stack is deep, so the pot odds to call are attractive. I will call to 300 total (add 200) to see a flop and try to realize equity. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet preflop, on the flop play cautiously out of position — continue only with a pair or a strong draw, otherwise check/fold to sustained aggression.

I have middle pair with the top kicker (Ah Jh) on a board of Qd-Jd-3h. This is a relatively strong hand in a three-handed game, but the board is coordinated with both straight and flush possibilities. I will lead with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from draws like diamond flushes, straight draws (KT, T9), and potentially weaker Jacks, while also gaining information and protecting my hand.

I called preflop with Td6s intending to fold the flop unless I hit a pair or a strong draw. Flop Jd Qd 3h gives me no pair and only weak backdoor outs (one diamond in hand for runner-runner flush and two-card straight possibilities). Flash's 300 into a 900 pot is a ~1/3-pot c-bet giving me ~25% pot odds to call, but my realistic equity to improve to a winning hand vs a continuing c-bet range is below that and I'm out of position. Calling invites further pressure on turn/river from hands that already connect with this board (Qx, Jx, Ax, broadways). Fold and preserve my 11.6k stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with AK suited (Clubs), which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. My current stack is healthy at 17,700. Minni is in the Small Blind and Elon is in the Big Blind. Standard play here is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot and take control of the hand in position. This puts pressure on the blinds and sets up a favorable post-flop scenario for my strong holdings.

Q♦J♣ (QJ offsuit) in the small blind facing a button open to 300. This is a defendable hand and the additional cost (250) is small relative to my 11,550 stack, so I want to see a flop and realize equity. 3-betting from the SB here would put me out of position postflop and is likely to either fold out only the weakest hands (if tiny) or get called/4-bet by the BB — not ideal with QJ. Calling keeps my range wide, keeps pot control, and lets me evaluate the flop; plan to proceed cautiously OOP, fold to heavy action when missed, and apply pressure only with strong equity or good reads.

KTo is a strong speculative hand out of the big blind facing a button raise from Flash, who tends to open wide from late position based on history (e.g., Hands 20,26,29,32,34,38). Minni's call from the small blind is loose, as seen in multiple spots (e.g., Hands 15,22,34). Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 more into a 700 pot (~3.5:1), requiring ~22% equity, which KTo exceeds multiway due to top-pair potential (K), broadway straight draws, and overcard value. Deep stacks (~100bb) favor realizing equity postflop rather than folding or 3-betting light into two opponents. Calling keeps the pot multiway, exploits wide ranges, and maintains balance.

Preflop I called with QJo to see a flop OOP and realize equity — that plan stays the same. Flop A♥ 9♦ 4♦ is a fairly dry A-high board with two diamonds. I have Q♥J♣: no pair, no diamond draw and very little equity versus the raiser’s opening range (and the BB cold-caller). This is a multiway spot out of position, so I want to control the pot and avoid getting committed with a marginal hand. Check and see what the button does. If faced with a small, probe-sized c-bet I can consider a float, but I will fold to a sizable continuation bet — otherwise I’ll check through and re-evaluate on the turn, looking to apply pressure only on a very favorable card (Q/J/K/T) or if I pick up a diamond.

Flop Ah9d4d misses my KTo completely - no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw, just king-high overcard value. Multiway pot (3 players, 900 effective) with a strong Ace-high board favoring Flash's wide button range (often Ax, broadway) and Minni's loose calling range. Minni's check indicates weakness (no strong Ax or sets likely to check). Betting here as a pure bluff is suboptimal: low fold equity multiway against sticky opponents, high likelihood of facing calls from Ax/draws or raises from monsters. Checking controls pot, realizes thin equity from overcards/live cards on clean turns, and avoids bloating pot OOP with air. Will fold to heavy aggression but call small bets if odds warrant. Balances prior calling range.

I have flopped Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King on an Ace-high board. Both opponents have checked to me, the pre-flop aggressor. While the board is somewhat dry, there are two diamonds present, making a flush draw possible. I need to bet for value against weaker Aces, nines, or pocket pairs, and to charge any diamond draws. A bet of 450 (50% of the pot) is a standard continuation bet that accomplishes these goals while maintaining pot control.

I call 450. I hold QhJc and completely missed the flop A♥ 9♦ 4♦ with two diamonds on board — no pair and no diamond draw. Flash’s bet is small (450 into a 1,350 pot), giving me ~20% break‑even equity to continue, and the price to float is attractive: calling is low risk relative to my 11,300 stack. Raising out of position with no pair or draw is a losing line (very little fold equity and a good chance of getting 3‑bet or priced in on later streets), and folding would be too tight given the cheap price and Flash’s frequent preflop aggression/small c‑bets. Plan after the call: try to see a turn and either realize equity (pair a Q/J) or attempt a positional bluff/pressure on safe turn cards; if I completely miss the turn and Flash fires again, I will fold unless I pick up a diamond or pair. If Elon shows aggression behind, I’ll re‑evaluate and fold to heavy action — but for now call and control the pot.

Facing a button half-pot bet into a checked-through flop after Minni's flat call in a 3-way pot of 1800, with KTo (king-high overcards, no pair/draws) on an A-high diamond board. Flash's button raise range includes frequent Ax (A9s/A4s suited connectors, broadway), flush draws (e.g., KdQd/JdTd), and pairs, hitting this board strongly ~40-50% of the time. Minni's loose calling history (Hands 15,22,34,39,41) suggests she has Ax, middle pairs (99/44 rare), gutshots, or weak draws that stick around. My equity is ~18-22% multiway (per quick sims: vs Ax+FD range ~20%; drops if monsters in mix), borderline for calling 450 into 2250 (~4:1 odds need 20%). However, reverse implied odds loom large OOP: turns/rivers likely worsen board (any diamond kills, pair/improver favors opponents), and I'll face further aggression on ~70% of streets from ranges that beat me. Bluff-raising has poor fold equity multiway (Minni sticky, Flash polarized bettor), risks stacks deep. Folding preserves stack for better spots; consistent with flop plan to control pot and fold to aggression. Long-term EV positive to avoid spewing ~450 chips here.

The turn (Qs) pairs my Qh — I’ve improved to top pair with a decent kicker (QJ). Flash is the button opener and made a small c‑bet on the A‑9‑4 flop, which is consistent with a wide/bet‑heavy range that contains many worse Qx/KQ/ pocket pairs and plenty of bluffs, but also a lot of Ax that currently beat me. With Elon folded I’m heads‑up and this is a good card to extract value and deny equity. I’ll bet for value and protection rather than check and give a free river card. A half‑pot sizing (~50% pot = 900) should get called by worse hands (Qx, KQ, medium pairs, some Jx) and charge backdoor diamonds; it keeps the pot controlled if called. If Flash raises sharply, I’ll assume a strong Ace/monster and fold; if he just calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

I have Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-King. Minni has lead into me on the Turn (a 'donk bet') after check-calling the Flop. The Queen of Spades is a somewhat coordinated card as it completes AQ, which is firmly in Minni's calling range. However, she could also be doing this with a weaker Ace (AJ, AT), a semi-bluff with a diamond draw, or even a smaller two-pair like A9 or A4. Raising here likely folds out her bluffs and weaker Aces while getting called by hands that beat me (sets or AQ). By calling, I keep the pot manageable, maintain position, and allow her to continue betting on the river with a wider range of hands that I currently beat.

Check. I bet the turn (900) and was called; the river 5♦ now brings a third diamond and substantially increases the portion of Flash’s calling range that now beats me (A♦x, K♦x, completed flushes). I hold top pair Qh with a medium kicker out of position — a decent but vulnerable showdown hand. Betting here risks bloating the pot and getting called (or raised) by hands that beat me, while producing little fold equity versus likely Ax and diamond holdings. Checking controls the pot, allows me to call a reasonable river stab from missed draws and fold to large aggression, and otherwise take a showdown with a marginal but often winning hand. Pot 3600, my stack ~9950, so preserving stack size and avoiding marginal bloats is the priority.

The river card is the 5 of Diamonds, which completes the diamond flush draw. Minni checked after leading the turn for 900. My Top Pair, Top Kicker (Ace-King) has significant showdown value, but the board is now quite dangerous with a possible flush and the turn card (Queen) potentially giving Minni two pair (AQ). When she checks after having led the turn, it's often a sign of caution or a 'check-call' line with a medium-strength hand. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands than mine, as she might fold her weaker Aces, but I will almost certainly be called or raised by any flush or two-pair. Since I have a strong showdown hand and there is no more value to be gained by betting into a board that completed several draws, checking back is the safest and most profitable play.

I'm on the button with J♦7♣ — a marginal offsuit hand but well within a button opening range against only the blinds. Raising for position and fold equity is the highest EV line here. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB). This size is large enough to put pressure on the blinds and not give them great odds to call, while still small relative to our ~99BB stack. Postflop plan: if called, use position to c-bet small on dry textures and control pot size on coordinated boards; fold to large, isolating 3-bets from aggressive opponents and only continue vs small 3-bets when the price and read justify it. Goal is to steal the blinds or play a single-raised pot in position.

Pocket sixes is a premium hand pre-flop, strong enough for a 3-bet against a button open-raise, which is typically wide-ranging. Minni has been aggressive with many pre-flop raises in the history, often with speculative hands, so she may fold to a 3-bet. This builds the pot OOP while having fold equity against her and potential squeeze vs BB. If called, 66 has good equity multiway or heads-up, with set-mining potential and overpair value. Raising to 900 (3x the open) is a standard sizing to apply pressure while keeping pot manageable with my stack.

I am holding J5o in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button and Elon 3-bet from the Small Blind. This hand is significantly below the calling range for a 3-bet, especially while being out of position against two active opponents. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here to preserve my chip lead.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♦7♣ and Elon (SB) has 3‑bet to 900. To call is 600 into a 1300 pot, so I need ~600/(1300+600) ≈ 31.6% equity to break even. J7o is weak and poorly positioned versus a standard 3‑bet range (AX, broadways, mid‑pairs) and will be dominated frequently. A 4‑bet bluff to the 1500 minimum is committing chips with a very marginal hand and a thin fold equity edge; calling to play postflop in position is possible vs very wide 3‑betters but Elon’s line and past aggression suggest a reasonably strong range here. My earlier plan was to fold to large isolating 3‑bets — that’s the correct low‑variance, +EV line here. I’ll fold and preserve my ~96BB stack for better spots.

9c2d is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em: offsuit, unconnected, low cards with no high card strength or suited potential. On the button with 108 chips BB effective stack (108bb deep), but position doesn't justify playing trash like this preflop. Opponents have shown aggressive tendencies in history (Minni and Flash often raise/limp and defend), but no action yet beyond blinds. Folding preserves stack for better spots; calling 100 into 150 pot (~22% pot odds) requires ~30% equity minimum, which 92o doesn't have vs any reasonable range. No steal incentive with BB to act behind.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Elon has folded on the button, leaving only me and Minni (Big Blind). Jack-Six offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, even in a three-handed game. Minni has shown significant aggression and willingness to defend her blinds in previous hands. Raising with such a weak hand risks being three-bet or called and having to play a poor hand out of position post-flop. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am holding AQo on the button in a three-handed game, which is a very strong hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative, aiming to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a range advantage.

I’m in the SB with K♠T♣ facing the BTN open to 300. KTo is not a premium but has decent playability and blocker value versus the button’s wide opening range. Calling 250 would too often leave me multiway and out of position; folding is too passive. I want to isolate the opener and take the initiative, so I’ll 3-bet to a meaningful size (≈4x the open) to 1,200 total. That sizing gives me fold equity vs weaker broadways and small pairs and discourages the BB cold-call while keeping enough stack behind to play post‑flop. If Flash 4-bets large I’ll concede this spot (KTo is often dominated), but if he calls I’ll c-bet or proceed postflop depending on texture.

K7o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position facing a large 3-bet (to 1200, 4x the button open) from the aggressive SB Minni. Button Flash may continue, making it multiway. Pot odds are not sufficient given the likely strong ranges (premium pairs, AK+ from SB), and my equity is low (~20-30% max vs. those ranges). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game.

I opened AQo on the button, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Minni's 3-bet from the small blind is a standard play. While I could 4-bet for value or to end the hand immediately, I've decided to call. Calling allows me to play in position against a range that likely includes dominated hands like AJ, AT, or KQ, and some bluffs that might fold to a 4-bet. This keeps the pot manageable and leverages my positional advantage post-flop.

I 3-bet preflop to 1,200 with K♠T♣ and got called on the button; pot is 2,500. The flop 2♥ 6♠ 2♣ is very dry and low — most of Flash’s calling range (broadways, Axs, many Kx) missed this board. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet to leverage fold equity and take the initiative. K♠ is a useful blocker to some Kx/Ax combos, and I have showdown value versus pure air, so a modest bet is optimal. I’ll bet ~1/3–40% of the pot (900) to fold out missed broadways while keeping the pot controllable if I run into a set (22/66) or a slow-played pocket pair. Plan: fold to a large raise, and if called re-evaluate the turn — continue barreling on safe bricks, check for pot control if the turn brings scary cards.

On a very dry board of 2-6-2, my Ace-Queen offsuit remains a strong non-pair hand that holds significant equity against Minni's 3-bet range. A 900 bet into a 2500 pot (roughly 1/3 pot) is a standard continuation bet. Folding here would be overly conservative given that I have two overcards and the board texture is unlikely to have improved most of her range significantly, unless she holds a pocket pair. I will call to see the turn and utilize my position to react to her future actions.

I 3‑bet preflop with K♠T♣ and c‑bet the dry 2♥6♠2♣ flop; Flash called my small flop stab. The turn 3♣ is a relatively safe brick — it doesn’t complete straights or flushes and only meaningfully helps a few combos (pocket 3s, 43/53 types). Most of the BTN’s calling range (Axs, Kx, broadways, small pairs) still missed. As the preflop aggressor I should continue applying pressure to fold out ace‑highs and non‑pair Kx/broadway hands while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll fire a modest barrel of 1,500 into the 4,300 pot (~35%) to get folds and define his range. If he raises big I’ll concede (KTo is often dominated); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and exercise pot control unless I improve or the river is another very safe brick that merits another bluff/value mix.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit on a very dry board of 2-6-2-3. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and has followed up with two small-sized continuation bets (roughly 1/3 pot on both the flop and the turn). My Ace-high still has significant showdown value against her potential bluffs or suited connectors that missed, and I am currently splitting with her own AQ or AK holdings. The price to call (1500 into a pot of 5800) gives me excellent pot odds (about 20%). Given the board texture and her betting pattern, I will call the turn bet to see the river and potentially win the pot at showdown or take it away if she checks on the river.

I check. Line review: I 3‑bet pre, c‑bet the dry 2♥6♠2♣ flop and barreled the 3♣ turn; Flash called both streets. The pot is 7,300 and my remaining stack is 6,100. The river A♣ changes things — it gives lots of Ax combinations top pair and completes a three‑club board that can make flushes. Flash’s tendency to call my flop and turn barrels points toward a calling range of Axs, broadways and medium pairs — many of which now beat my K♠T♣. I block one club and one king, but not nearly enough to dissuade the likelihood he has an A or a club draw. Betting as a bluff here would need to fold out Ax and decent pairs, but given his passive calls he’s likely to call or raise with those hands; to fold him I’d have to commit a very large portion of my stack (high variance and thin). Therefore the prudent line is to check and fold to any meaningful river bet; if he checks back I take showdown but expect to be behind vs most value hands.

The river Ace is an excellent card for my hand, as I have now paired my Ace with a Queen kicker. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and led out on both the flop and turn, but has now checked the river. This check often indicates a hand like KK, QQ, or JJ that is now concerned about the Ace, or a marginal Ace like AJ/AT. While I could bet for value, there is a risk she is checking a stronger Ace like AK or a slow-played monster to induce a bluff. Given the pot size and the fact that I have strong showdown value, checking back is the safest way to ensure I win the pot against her weaker range while avoiding a potential check-raise from the top of her range. I will take the free showdown.

I'm on the button with Q♦8♥ in a 3-handed pot. In this spot I should open a fairly wide range — Q8o is fine to raise for value and fold equity. I want a standard open that gives the blinds bad odds to call and preserves postflop playability in position, so I will raise to 300 (3xBB). Size rationale: 300 is large enough to generate fold equity and avoid cheap multiway pots but small enough to keep the pot manageable with 61bb effective stacks. Postflop plan: if called I'll use position to c‑bet favourable high-card or dry textures and proceed cautiously on coordinated/connected boards; if I face a significant 3‑bet (much larger than ~2.5x my open) Q8o is usually too weak and I'll fold, while I can consider calling small 3‑bets to play in position against the BB. Overall opening to 300 maximizes expected value from the blinds while keeping options for postflop play.

A9s is a strong suited ace hand with good playability and blockers to strong aces. Facing a button raise to 300 from Minni, who raises frequently based on history (often with marginal hands and wins small pots). Excellent pot odds: calling 250 into 450 (1.8:1 direct, plus implied odds given stacks). OOP but can realize equity well post-flop with flush potential, top pair potential, or strong draws. 3-betting risks OOP bloating pot with a non-nutted hand; calling keeps pot multi-way possible with BB Flash (deep stack) and plays well post-flop. Folding too tight pre-flop.

With Jack-Five offsuit, I am facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind. Although the pot odds are attractive (calling 200 to win a total pot of 700), this hand is structurally weak and plays poorly out of position. To maintain a solid strategy and avoid difficult post-flop scenarios with a dominated hand, I will choose to fold.

Post-flop heads-up OOP with A♦9♠ on 7♣2♣J♥ board. No pair, no draws, pure ace-high bluff-catcher. Flop is relatively dry but with club flush draw and some straight draw possibilities (e.g., 65, T8). Minni raised button pre-flop (her frequent spot) and has position, so she'll c-bet wide for value/thin value or bluffs. Betting out risks getting raised off best hand or called by better; checking allows pot control, checks behind possible (though unlikely), or facing a bet I can evaluate with ace-high. Good implied odds pre-flop justified the call, but now play passively to control pot and fold to aggression unless small bet. Long-term EV positive by not bloating pot OOP with air.

I opened to 300 preflop and was called. On 7♣ 2♣ J♥ Elon checked to me. Q♦8♥ missed the flop but has a Q overcard and I'm the preflop raiser in position, so a continuation bet is the default — it folds out many worse hands and gets value/protection vs club draws and weaker Jx. The board is relatively dry (just a two‑club backdoor draw and one high card), so I'll use a small-to-medium c‑bet to maximize fold equity without bloating the pot: betting ~300 (~40–45% pot). Plan: fold to a large raise, call small flatraises only with a clear plan to proceed cautiously on the turn, and if called I'll control pot size and look to take advantage of my position on later streets.

Facing a 300 bet into 1000 pot (30% pot) OOP on 7c2cJh flop with Ad9s (ace-high, no pair, no draw). Minni's button c-bet range is wide (Jx, 87, sets, Ax, bluffs), and my hand has poor playability post-flop OOP. Equity realization low; ace-high rarely best and vulnerable to turns. Pre-flop call was correct for implied odds, but pot control check met with bet warrants folding marginal bluff-catcher. History shows Minni value-bets thin but also folds to aggression; no need to continue spew. Preserve stack for better spots.

Js3h is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and playability postflop. On the button in a 3-handed game, but facing a 100 to call into a 150 pot (pot odds ~2.5:1 if calling), the equity against an opponent's raising range (likely BB after SB fold) is insufficient (~30-40% at best, probably less). Opponents like Minni raise frequently from blinds but folding trash preserves stack for better spots in long game (150 hands). No strong reason to speculate here.

I am in the Small Blind with Q2o. The Button (Elon) has folded. The pot is currently 150 chips (100 from Minni's BB and 50 from my SB). It is only 50 chips more for me to call. Given that it's a Blind vs. Blind situation and Minni has been quite active, calling to see a flop is a reasonable low-cost option, though folding is also standard with such a weak hand. However, to maintain some table presence and given the pot odds (3:1), I will complete the small blind.

I have 4♦9♥ — a weak, unsuited hand with poor playability. Flash just limped from the SB, and I’ve already got 100 in (BB) so I’ll take the free/flop option. No reason to enlarge the pot or isolate with this hand given Flash’s tendency to call and my shorter stack (6,400 vs Flash’s 23,000). Plan: check to see the flop; fold to any sizable continuation bet if I miss, call small bets with backdoor draws or a pair, and only invest further with a real piece of the board or a clear fold equity spot.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-high flop after completing the Small Blind and checking. Minni, in the Big Blind, also checked. The board is dry, but the presence of the Ace makes it likely one of us has a piece or is waiting to bluff. Since I have no pair and no real draw, I will check to see how Minni reacts. If she bets, I will likely fold as Q-high has very little showdown value here.

Check. I have 9♥4♦ on A♥8♥3♣ — no pair and only a single heart (a very remote backdoor flush). Flash just limped (likely a wide, calling range) and I’m the shorter stack, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Checking controls the pot size and preserves options. Plan: fold to any sizable C‑bet, call a very small C‑bet (≈≤1/3–1/2 pot) to realize the backdoor heart/pair equity, and reassess on the turn if seen.

On the turn, I have improved to bottom pair with a pair of Deuces. The flop went check-check on an Ace-high board, suggesting Minni likely doesn't have an Ace. However, since the pot is very small and my hand is still quite weak (bottom pair, weak kicker), I will check again. This allows me to see a river for free and potentially reach a cheap showdown. If Minni bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size with a marginal hand.

Check. I hold 9♥4♦ on A♥8♥3♣2♦ — no pair and my backdoor heart draw is gone (the turn is a diamond, so a river heart cannot give me a 5‑card flush). I have only small pair equity (three 9s and three 4s ≈13% to pair on the river), so I don’t want to blo﻿w off chips by barreling into a limper who checks twice and is likely to call light. Checking controls the pot (200) and preserves my shorter stack (6,400) while keeping the option to call a small bet on the river to realize equity. Plan: check the turn, fold to any sizable turn bet, call a very small bet (~≤1/3–1/2 pot) to see the river, and reassess if the river comes.

The river is a 6s, making the final board Ah-8h-3c-2d-6s. Action has gone check-check on all streets so far. I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a Queen kicker. In a limped pot where the Big Blind (Minni) has checked three times, my pair of Deuces actually has a decent chance of being the best hand. Minni might have a missed draw or a very weak high card. Since the pot is tiny (200 chips) and there is very little value in betting (better hands like a weak Ace or an 8 won't fold, and worse hands will rarely call), I will check to see the showdown and take my chances with my pair.

Board: A♥ 8♥ 3♣ 2♦ 6♠. I hold 9♥4♦ — no pair, no completed straight, and my backdoor heart is gone. Flash limped preflop and checked flop, turn and river, showing a very passive/wide checking range (could be A‑x, small pair, or just missed draws). Pot is tiny (200) and I am the short stack (6,400). A river bluff here has poor risk/reward — many of Flash’s hands will call a small bet and better hands will call or raise; I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Consistent with my earlier plan to check and only invest with real equity or to call very small bets, I check back and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8h 2d, which is a very weak hand. The pot is only 150 and I am facing a small call of 100, but with a stack of 23,200, there is no reason to play garbage hands from the button, especially since Minni and Elon are still to act behind me. Standard play is to fold.

Heads-up from the small blind with Qc8c. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (100 total) to see a flop — that gives ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity). Q8s is a playable, suited broadway with good postflop playability and backdoor flush/straight potential, but it’s not strong enough to make a standard min-raise vs an opponent who 3-bets a lot; a min-raise would just open me to larger aggression OOP. I’ll defend the blind by calling to realize equity and keep the pot manageable OOP. Plan: evaluate the flop — continue with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand preflop.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind limp after button fold. My hand 8d4s is weak, offsuit with no connectivity or high card strength, making it unsuitable for raising or bluffing here. Checking to see a flop for free is standard BB defense strategy against a SB limp, preserving stack while taking advantage of position and implied odds if I hit. No need to fold since it's free money already in the pot.

I have top pair with Qc8c on Qh-4h-2s. In heads-up I’m in position (I act last postflop) so I can both extract value and control the pot. Elon checked preflop (showing a relatively wide/weak checking range), and the board has two hearts so there are some heart draw combos I want to charge/protect against. A half-pot bet (~100 into 200) gets value from worse queens, small pairs and some calls from draws while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance. Plan: bet 100; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously with my mediocre kicker; if he check-raises large I’ll usually fold to avoid bloating the pot out of position against a polarized range.

Facing a small 100 bet into 300 pot from SB limper (pot odds ~4:1, need ~20% equity). Hold bottom pair 4s with 8 kicker on Qh4h2s board. No strong draws but paired, decent equity vs wide SB limp-bet range (Ax, mid pairs, draws, bluffs). OOP but pot controlled, effective stacks 62BB/103BB allow seeing turn cheaply to realize equity or improve. Raising risks too weak; folding too tight given price. Standard call to pot control and extract value if ahead.

I have top pair (Qc) with an 8 kicker. I barreled the flop (100) and Elon called, and the turn 6d is a relatively harmless card — it doesn’t complete many straights and only preserves heart-draw combos. Pot is 400 and he checked to me. I want to extract value from worse Qx and medium pairs (4x/2x) and charge any two-heart flush draws, but I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker. A half-pot bet (~200) is the right balance: it prices 9‑out heart draws incorrectly (they have ~19.6% to hit the river but would need ~25% equity to profitably call 200 into the 600 pot), gets value from worse pairs/top pairs, and keeps the pot controlled relative to my 6,200 stack. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he check-raises big I’ll fold to avoid getting stacked by a polarized range.

Pot is 600, facing a 200 bet (pot odds ~3:1, requiring ~25% equity). Holding bottom pair (4s) with 8 kicker on Qh-4h-2s-6d board. The 6d is somewhat blank but completes 3-5 straights, keeping flush draws live. Minni's SB limp range is wide, flop minbet and now small turn bet (~1/3 pot) consistent with weak pairs, draws, or floats rather than strong value like Qx or sets (which might bet larger). My pair has showdown value against much of her range and decent equity (~35-45% vs polarized range). Deep effective stacks (min 60BB), position post-turn allows cheap realization of equity or river control. Raising risks commitment with marginal hand and fold equity not worth it; folding premature given price and history of Minni's pot control. Standard call to keep pot small while staying in with live hand.

I have Qc8c — top pair with a weak kicker. I led the flop (100 into 200) and barreled the turn (200 into a 400 pot); Elon called both streets, which looks like a calling/down range of worse queens, second pairs (4x/2x) and some draws. The river (7d) is fairly innocuous — it doesn’t complete obvious straights or a heart flush (the two hearts on the flop didn’t get there) and only pairs a few hands. With showdown value and a caller who has shown willingness to call down, I should extract value rather than check and give up value to worse Qx and medium pairs. I’ll size value-bet about half the pot to get calls from worse Qs and single pairs while avoiding bloating the pot if I’m behind. If Elon check‑raises big I’ll respect a polarized range and fold. Action: bet ~1/2 pot (400).

River facing 400 bet into 1200 pot (~3.75:1 odds, ~21% equity needed). Holding bottom pair 4s with 8d kicker on Qh4h2s6d7d board. No improvement but showdown value vs Minni's SB limp-call range (wide: Ax suited, low-mid pairs, draws). Her small flop/turn bets (100/200) then 1/2-pot river suggests thin value (weak Qx, low pairs) or semi-bluff rather than nuts (sets, two-pair would bet larger earlier). 7d river completes some wheel-ish straights (e.g., 35 for 34567) or diamond draws, but SB limp range assigns low probability to those; hearts flush missed. Equity ~35-45% vs polarized range sufficient for call. Raising risks weak kicker vulnerability OOP; folding too tight given price/history of Minni pot control. Call realizes equity cheaply, controls pot size with deep stacks (me 100BB, her ~50BB). Consistent with prior streets.

Holding 4♣4h? Wait: 4h 2s — one of the weakest hands. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop, meaning I need ~40% equity (100/(150+100)≈40%) to justify a call. 4‑2 offsuit has essentially no multiway equity or postflop playability against the SB and BB ranges here. I could attempt a light raise to steal, but both opponents are deep and have shown willingness to defend/3‑bet — I’d be putting myself in difficult spots out of position for minimal fold equity. With a 7200 stack I should conserve chips and wait for better opportunities on the button. Fold.

Preflop, unopened pot (Minni folded), in SB with A6o vs BB. A6o is a standard SB raise vs BB as it blocks AA/AK and has decent equity (~60% vs random range). SB should widen range to steal blinds frequently. Standard raise size 3bb (300 total). Limping is poor due to position; folding too tight. Opponents fold to preflop aggression often, building stack over 150 hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 73s. The button folded and Elon (Small Blind) raised to 300. While I am in position post-flop and the hand is suited, 7d3d is extremely weak and easily dominated by Elon's opening range. Given the 3x raise size, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than defending a marginal hand that often realizes its equity poorly.

3-handed on the button with J6s, a suited connector with good playability and position. SB limped, BB posted. Pot 150, to call 100. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard to steal blinds/antes effectively, isolate the limper if weak, or build pot OOP for postflop advantage. J6s flops well often (straights, flushes, pairs), and deep stacks allow maneuvering. Opponents defend wide based on history but fold pre often to button raises. Folding too tight, calling passive - prefer initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with QTo, a solid semi-premium hand in a 3-handed game. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. QTo is well ahead of a typical button opening range. While I could 3-bet to take control of the hand, calling is a safer option that keeps his weaker range in and allows me to play post-flop with a hand that has good connectivity and high-card strength. Minni in the Big Blind is still to act, but my hand is strong enough to continue even if she squeezes. I will call the 250 to see a flop.

I’m in the big blind with 4c7d facing a Button raise to 300 and the small blind called — pot is 700 and I must call 200. Calling 200 into an eventual 900 pot requires ≈22% equity to break even, and 4-7 offsuit has very poor multiway equity and postflop playability, especially out of position against two opponents. A bluff-raise is possible in some spots, but here both players are deep and the raiser is frequent and likely ahead; this would be high variance and low EV. Fold and conserve my stack for better spots.

On a board of 9s-Ks-8c, I have a gutshot straight draw (to a Jack). Elon raised pre-flop from the button and Flash (me) called from the Small Blind. The flop hits a lot of a button opening range, but also interacts well with my QTo. Since I am out of position and Elon was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to see how he continues. I have some equity with the gutshot and overcards, but not enough to lead out into the aggressor on this texture.

Flop of 9s Ks 8c with Js6s gives me the nut spade flush draw (Js blocks Qs/As somewhat) plus a gutshot straight draw to the 10 for Broadway (8-9-T-J-K). Approximately 13-15 outs for two overs, massive equity (~45%+ against pairs/top pair, even vs sets). Flash checks from SB into my button raise, range likely wide/capped (Ax suited, pairs, broadways that missed). Position allows control. Betting half-pot builds pot for when we hit, extracts value from worse draws/pairs/Kx, denies equity to runner-runner straights. Size 350 denies cheap turns effectively without overbetting. If check-raised, strong hand/reevaluate jam with stack.

On this flop of 9s-Ks-8c, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (with a Jack) and one overcard (the Queen). Elon has made a half-pot continuation bet of 350 into 700. My pot odds are exactly 3-to-1, meaning I need 25% equity to justify a call. In a 3-handed game, Elon's button-opening range is wide, and while this board hits him well, Q-high with a gutshot has enough equity (roughly 25-30% against a standard range) and implied odds if I hit the straight to continue for one street. I will call to see if the turn improves my hand.

The turn card is the Queen of Diamonds, giving me second pair (Queens) and an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or nine gives me a straight, though the nine is already on the board). I have significantly improved my equity. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked back on the turn after his flop continuation bet was called. This check often indicates either a medium-strength hand (like a King with a weak kicker) or a total air hand that has given up. By checking, I am giving Elon the opportunity to bluff or checking behind to see a free river where I can potentially improve to a straight or two pair. Given the board is quite coordinated and I have showdown value now, checking is a standard play to control the pot size and realize my equity.

Nut spade flush draw remains live with 9 outs (~19% equity on turn). Board 9s-Ks-8c-Qd is coordinated: possible Broadway straights (T-J), KQ/AQ improved, but Flash's double check (pre-flop call from SB, flop check-call, turn check) caps his range at medium pairs (88-99, JJ-TT), Kx (weak kickers/draws), weaker spade draws (Axs below Js, 7s5s etc.), or missed broadways. No strong combos likely (sets would raise flop, KK unlikely cold-call pre). Position allows betting for thin value from worse pairs/draws/K9-KT, deny equity to runner-runner straights/flushes/hearts, build pot for river jam if spade hits or thin value shove. Checking risks giving free river to outs. Opponents call stations historically (e.g., Hands 47,50), deep stacks support semi-bluff. Size ~2/3 pot (900 into 1400) balances value/protection without overcommitting.

On the turn, I have improved to second pair with a Queen, and I also hold a gutshot straight draw (needing any Jack to make a straight). Elon's bet of 900 into a 1400 pot represents strength, likely a King or better, but in a 3-handed game, second pair with a strong kicker and a nut-straight draw is too valuable to fold. My pot odds are approximately 2.5-to-1, and my combined equity from the pair and the draw justifies a call to see the river and potentially improve or win at showdown.

The river is a Deuce of Diamonds, which is a total brick. The board is 9s-Ks-8c-Qd-2d. I have second pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker. On the turn, Elon bet 900 and I called. On the river, the action is now on me after the 2d completed the board. I checked (as shown by the previous log entry's absence of action from me before Elon's turn action, implying I checked the river to the aggressor). Looking at the log, Elon has not bet the river yet. I am the Small Blind and checked to Elon. If he checks back, I likely have the best hand against his bluffs or missed draws (like JT or spade draws). If he bets, I have a tough decision, but since the current state shows 'To Call: 0' and no river bet from Elon has been recorded in the current hand log after the 2d, the action remains on me to check or bet. Given I have showdown value with second pair and the board is coordinated, checking is the best play to reach showdown cheaply.

River 2d is a blank that doesn't complete any new strong draws relevant to Flash's check-call range (spade flush draw missed, diamond flush unlikely given no prior diamonds on board and his check-calls). My Js6s missed the nut spade flush draw and gutshot entirely, leaving me with jack-high and no showdown value. Flash's range for check-calling flop (350 into ~950 pot) and turn (900 into ~2000+) is capped: medium pairs (88-99, JJ/TT overpairs unlikely), weak Kx (KJ/KT/K9), backdoor draws that improved minimally, or weaker spade draws (7s5s, 10s7s etc.) without the nuts. Sets/KK/AK/KQ would likely raise earlier streets. Strong hands like KQ/K9 two-pair or straights (e.g., T7 unlikely in range) are possible but thin. His river check suggests no monster, avoiding value betting thin showdown hands or trapping. Double-barreling as preflop raiser reps top pair+, Broadway, or strong draws hit. However, bluffing here risks calling station tendencies (e.g., Hands 29,47,50 where Flash/Minni called multiple streets light), huge pot/stack disparity (3200 pot, my 8150 stack), and low fold equity vs capped range that can hero-call worse. Checking preserves stack for superior spots in 95+ hands remaining, realizes equity by showing down jack-high occasionally (beats pure air/bluffs in his range), avoids spew. Long-term EV maximized by not over-bluffing passive checkers.

I am on the button with Ace-high (Ah 4c) in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening range hand. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and pressure the blinds, which has been a consistent opening size in this game.

2♠7♠ is the worst starting hand — essentially never ahead of a button's 3x open. Calling 250 into a 450 pot gives me ~35.7% pot‑odds, but I have far less equity vs a typical raising range and I'll be out of position post‑flop. With deep stacks and plenty of better spots to invest in, folding preserves my stack and avoids a negative‑EV situation.

3-handed, BB vs BTN open. Button ranges are very wide in this spot, especially given Flash's frequent raising history from previous hands. J2s is suited, has straight and flush potential, good playability and implied odds deep-stacked (effective ~8k). Pot odds are excellent: 200 to call into 450 (pot will be 650 after call, ~3.25:1). Defending wide OOP vs BTN is standard. Folding too tight; raising not strong enough for value/3bet bluff here. Call and realize equity multiway potential or heads-up OOP but with postflop potential.

Top pair with a deuce kicker on a J97 rainbow-ish board, OOP heads-up vs Button raiser with a wide range. Hand has decent showdown value, blocks top pair (Jh blocks one J), and backdoor heart flush potential. Pot is 650, stacks deep (~78bb effective). First to act: betting risks getting raised by better Js (KQ, AJ, etc.) or sets/draws, and thin value. Better to check, induce bluffs/cbets from Flash's frequent aggression (seen in prior hands like #55, #50), and call a standard cbet profitably (~300-450 into 650 offers good odds). Fold to overbets or multiple streets. Realizes equity OOP without bloating pot with marginal strength.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-high (Ah 4c) and was called by Elon in the big blind. The flop comes Js-7d-9h, which is a fairly coordinated board that hits a lot of the big blind's calling range (hands like QJ, JT, T9, 87, and various straight draws). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative on many textures. My Ace-high has some showdown value but very little equity on this board if called. A continuation bet here can often take down the pot immediately against hands that missed, such as smaller pairs or pocket pairs like 22-66. Given the board texture, I will bet around half the pot to test Elon's strength.

Facing a standard ~1/2 pot cbet (325 into 975) OOP on J97 rainbow board with top pair weak kicker (Jh2h suited). Flash's BTN range is wide (Ax suited, broadway, connectors, bluffs), and history shows aggression (frequent raises/cbets in hands like #55, #50, #47). Hand blocks Js, has showdown value, backdoor flush draw, and deep effective stacks (~78bb) provide implied odds. Pot odds excellent (325 into 975 = ~3:1 immediate, plus future bets). Raising risks isolating vs better Js/sets/TPs while bloating pot OOP; folding too tight given equity realization vs cbet-heavy range. Call to pot control, induce further bluffs/overbets, realize equity multi-street.

Turn pairs the board with Jc, and holding Jh2h gives me trip Jacks (Js/Jc/Jh) with a 2 kicker, an extremely strong hand heads-up OOP vs Flash's wide button opening range. His range includes overpairs, weaker Js (AJ/KQss), straight draws (T8/T10), sets (77/99), two-pair combos, and bluffs/floats. Trips crush most of it and beat any non-nut straight/set. First to act on paired board: checking risks giving free cards to draws (e.g., 8T flushdraw/straight) or allowing bluffs/checks; instead, bet for value to build the pot while denying equity. Deep effective stacks (~75bb) allow multi-street value. Size ~2/3 pot (900 into 1300) balances value/targeting calls from worse (TPTK, draws) and induces raises from sets/AK which we snap-call/shove. Flash's history shows frequent aggression/calls (e.g., #55, #50, #47), so expect payment.

Elon's lead on the turn after the board pairs the Jack is a strong signal. Having called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, his range includes many Jacks (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT) that have now improved to trips. Even if he is semi-bluffing with a straight draw, my Ace-high has very little equity and no clear path to winning the hand without a significant bluff. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I have A♦K♦ on the button — a premium, suited Broadway hand. With only the blinds to act, I should open for value and fold equity rather than just calling. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (bigger than the min-raise to 200) charges the blinds' wide calling ranges, gains fold equity and keeps post‑flop maneuverability with a deep stack (7050). If I face a 3‑bet I'll re-evaluate (call reasonable 3‑bets to play postflop, 4‑bet/shove versus polarizing large 3‑bets depending on opponent). Open to 300.

J7 suited is a speculative hand with good playability, suited connectors that can flop strong draws or pairs. Facing a button raise, which is typically wide, from Minni. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 more (total 300 from me) into 450 pot (~1.8:1). Defending SB wide is standard in cash games vs loose button opens. BB may call or squeeze, but multiway pots are fine OOP with this hand. Folding is too tight preflop; raising lacks value and is risky OOP. Plan to call and reassess flop.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o. Minni raised to 3BB (300) from the Button, and Elon called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 3.5:1 on a call, and Q9o is strong enough to defend in a multi-way pot from the big blind against a button open and a small blind flat, as it has decent playability and high-card value. I will call and see a flop.

Flop Td Ah 9h completely misses my Jd7d. I have jack-high with no pair, no flush draw (three diamonds total), no straight draw (no gutshot or open-ender connecting well; J doesn't fill meaningfully on A-T-9). Board is very coordinated: two hearts for flush draw, A-high top pair potential, straight draws like KQ, Q8, J8. Preflop, Minni opened wide from late position (button), I defended speculatively OOP with suited connector, Flash BB defended wide. Multiway OOP with air on scary board, vulnerable to any bet from ranges that hit this often (Ax, draws). Betting risks getting check-raised or called by better; check-fold is standard to control pot and realize equity only if checked through (unlikely but possible). No fold needed yet as unbet pot, but pot committed low (~11% stack). Prioritize survival, reassess turn if free card.

I have bottom pair (nines) with a Queen kicker on a very coordinated board (Td-Ah-9h). There is an Ace and a Ten out there, plus a flush draw and several straight draws. Since Minni was the pre-flop raiser and Elon checked from the Small Blind, the most prudent move is to check and see how Minni reacts. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm likely only called by better hands or strong draws that have significant equity against me. Checking allows me to evaluate the strength of my hand relative to the action.

I have A♦K♦ — top pair with the best kicker on Td Ah 9h. Two hearts are on the board, so opponents can have strong heart-based draws (or slow-played two-pairs/sets), and many of their calling hands (weaker Ax, Tx, 9x, suited connectors) will pay me off. Both checked to me, so I should extract value and deny equity. With a pot of 900 I want a bet large enough to charge two-heart flush draws and worse Aces but still get called by my value hands — about 2/3 pot is appropriate. I will bet 600. If I get called I’ll continue in position and re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a strong check-raise I’ll assume a very strong hand and proceed cautiously (call small-to-medium raises, fold to polarizing overbets).

Preflop, Jd7d is a marginal suited connector defend from SB vs button open. Flop Td Ah 9h is a wet, coordinated board with A-top pair potential, heart flush draws, straight draws like KQ, Q8, J8. Minni's button range hits this hard (Ax often, suited broadways, draws). My jack-high has no pair, backdoor diamond flush draw at best, no meaningful straight draw (Jd sits awkwardly between T and A). Equity is poor vs Minni's C-bet range (~20-30% at best, less OOP). Pot 1500, call 600 commits ~7% stack into pot that grows, leaves me OOP multiway potentially vs Flash too. Check-fold is standard discipline on missed flop to avoid tough spots; no reason to bluff-raise air. Preserve stack for better spots.

On a board of Ad-Th-9h, I have bottom pair with a Queen kicker. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 600 into a pot of 900, which is a significant 2/3 pot sizing after Elon folded. This board is extremely coordinated, containing an Ace, several straight draws (KJ, QJ, 87), and a flush draw. While Q9 has some showdown value, it is very vulnerable here. Minni's range on the button is wide, but a 2/3 pot bet into two opponents usually indicates a strong made hand (like an Ace or a Ten) or a high-equity draw. Given my weak position and the likelihood that many turn cards will be difficult to play (any heart, King, Jack, or Eight), folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Ts7c is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low kicker potential. In 3-handed play from the button, opening range is wide but this is below standard playable range (e.g., prefer suited connectors, broadways, Ax). Opponents Flash and Minni frequently raise and defend aggressively based on history, risking difficult postflop spots OOP with marginal equity. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

In a blind-versus-blind situation with King-high, I have a range advantage and a hand that is typically ahead of the Big Blind's random holding. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Even if called, I maintain initiative and have decent high-card value.

I have A♣9♠ in the big blind facing a 300 open from the SB (200 more to call). Heads-up the SB will have a very wide opening range, so A9o has decent equity, and calling 200 into a 400 pot (pot after call = 600) requires ~33% equity — achievable vs a wide range. I prefer to flat-call rather than 3-bet: 3-betting takes me out of position postflop and commits more against a deeper-stacked, aggressive opener (Flash). Calling lets me realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and proceed based on the flop texture (value bet or fold/defend accordingly). Plan: call to 300 total, evaluate flop for continuation or pot-control.

On this dry, unconnected flop (7d 4h 2s), as the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-versus-blind battle, I have a significant range advantage. My opponent's calling range from the big blind often includes many hands that missed this board entirely. I'll lead with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain pressure and fold out better high-card hands or under-pairs that may be uncomfortable continuing on this texture.

Flop: 7♦ 4♥ 2♠. I hold A♣9♠ — I missed the board and currently only have Ace-high with no realistic draw. Flash’s 300 into a 900 pot is a small c-bet consistent with a very wide SB opening/continuation range (lots of missed overcards and bluffs). Calling 300 to potentially win ~1500 requires only ~20% equity, which A9o has vs a wide c-bet range. Calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity out-of-position; raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and invite difficult decisions if I get reraised. Folding is too weak since many of Flash’s continuing hands are bluffs or unpaired. Plan: flat-call the 300, see the turn, and re-evaluate (value bet/raise if I pair my Ace; fold or proceed cautiously if he barrels a scary card).

On the turn, the 3s improves my hand to second pair (threes) with a King kicker. The board is now 7d-4h-2s-3s, which is relatively disconnected, though a straight is possible (5-6). As the pre-flop aggressor who also led the flop, I should continue representing strength while also realizing that I now have some showdown value. If I check, I allow Minni to potentially bluff or take a free card. However, a small bet can keep the lead and charge potential draws or worse hands (like A-high or lower pairs) to continue. Given the pot size and the board texture, I will check here to control the pot and see how Minni reacts, as my pair of threes is vulnerable but potentially the best hand right now.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop — that line screams either a missed wide c-bet range or pot-control with a medium pair. I have A♣9♠ with no pair and no realistic draw on 7♦-4♥-2♠-3♠. Leading out out-of-position with a marginal Ace-high would bloat the pot unnecessarily and will often be called by the hands that beat me (pocket pairs, any 3/4/7 combos) while only folding out some overcards. With the pot at 1,200 and my stack relatively short compared to Flash, I want to keep the pot manageable, preserve fold equity, and see a cheap river where I can re-evaluate (value-bet if I hit an Ace, call small river bets, or fold to large pressure). Checking behind is the lowest-variance, highest-EV line here.

On the river, the King of Hearts completes the board. I have improved to King-high top pair (Kings and Threes). The board texture (7d 4h 2s 3s Kh) is very dry. My opponent checked back the turn, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a missed draw. With the King on the river, I have effectively moved from a marginal second-pair hand to the likely winning hand. I want to extract value from any Seven, small pocket pair, or Ace-high that might call a small-to-medium value bet. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot looks strong but is likely to be called by a Seven or a curious Ace-high.

I checked the river to keep the pot controlled with A♣9♠. Flash now fires 800 into the ~1,200 pot (2000 total); calling 800 requires ~40% equity. My hand is just Ace-high with no realistic redraws; I only beat pure bluffs and very few of Flash’s likely river continuation hands. Flash’s line (open pre, c-bet flop, check turn, now a ~0.67-pot river bet) is consistent with thin value hands (small pairs, Kx) or a polarizing steal — but the bet size is large enough that his bluff frequency would need to be very high for a call to be profitable. Raising as a bluff is unattractive (I have only one Ace blocker and would face tough decisions vs a deep, aggressive opponent). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am on the button with 82o, which is a very weak hand. The pot is currently small (150) with only 100 to call, but this hand has poor playability and is unlikely to win at showdown. I have a healthy stack and don't need to waste chips on marginal or bad holdings in this position.

Heads-up from the small blind with K♠T♥. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but KTo is a strong, playable hand heads-up and is a good candidate to open/steal. I’ll raise to the minimum (200) to take the initiative, deny the BB good pot odds to see a cheap flop, and keep the pot manageable out of position. With a 7k stack this is a small investment that preserves fold equity; if Elon 3-bets big I can reassess (likely fold to large 3-bets or proceed based on sizing/reads).

9s5d is a complete trash hand offsuit with poor playability, large gap, and dominated by most of Minni's stealing range from SB after BTN fold. Facing a raise to 200 (call 100 into 300 pot), pot odds are not sufficient given the poor equity (~25-35% vs wide SB open) and terrible postflop prospects OOP in BB. Minni raises frequently but folding weak hands preserves stack for better spots. No reason to defend this.

Three-handed on the button with 6s2s. In a 3-handed game the button should open very wide — 6-2s is marginal but has suitedness and positional advantage, so I'm taking the initiative rather than limping (which invites multiway action where this hand has poor equity). I will raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure and steal the blinds; the size is large enough to generate fold equity but small enough not to overcommit. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold; if called I'll c-bet or proceed cautiously on non-spade, high-card flops and realize equity when appropriate.

JThs is a strong speculative hand with suited connectors, good playability postflop for straights and flushes. Minni's button raise is likely wide based on history of frequent raising. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot offers decent implied odds with my 8375 stack and Minni's 6850. BB may call or raise, but multiway pots suit this hand OOP. Folding is too tight preflop; raising risks getting 3-bet OOP without position advantage.

I am in the big blind holding A6o. Minni raised to 3x from the button, and Elon called from the small blind. The pot is offering me 3.5:1 on a call (200 to win 700). A6o is a strong enough hand to defend against a button open, especially with the small blind coming along. Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and the strength of an Ace. Raising (3-betting) is an option, but given the stacks and the fact that Elon already called, taking a flop and playing post-flop seems more prudent here than bloating a pot with a mediocre kicker.

Top two pair (Jacks and Tens) with the top end of the straight draw heavy board (open-ended straight draws possible with 8x or Qx, backdoor flush draws). Multiway pot OOP after calling preflop with suited connectors that connected perfectly. Pot is 900; betting around 2/3 pot builds value from worse pairs/draws, protects against draws completing, and denies equity to free cards. Minni and Flash have shown aggression but folding to strength in spots; Flash deep but BB defends wide. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or check-raises. Strong value bet sizing to get called by 1-pair, weaker two-pair, or draws.

On a board of J-T-9, my Ace-high has almost zero equity against a lead from the small blind into two players. This board is extremely coordinated and hits almost every part of a standard calling or raising range (pairs, straights, and massive draws). Given that Elon led for 2/3 pot and Minni is still to act behind me, there is no reason to continue with a hand that has no showdown value and no draws. I will fold.

Preflop I opened wide from the button, but the flop is extremely unfavorable to 6♠2♠. J♣ 9♠ T♦ gives me no pair, no real straight draw and only a runner‑runner spade flush (very unlikely). Elon’s bet 600 into the 1500 pot means I need roughly ~22% equity to make a break‑even call. While the raw chance to pair one of my cards by river (~24%) looks close, most of those pair outs make weak second/third pair and will still lose to the kinds of hands Elon is betting (top pairs, tens, nines, broadway combinations and strong draws). Raising would be an overcommitted bluff with poor fold equity and bad stack leverage. Fold and wait for a better, more realizable equity spot.

3-handed on the button with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100). 87o is a playable hand from this position with good post-flop potential due to straight possibilities and suited blocker. Opponents have folded frequently to button raises in past hands (e.g., many steals successful). Raising to steal blinds or build pot in position. Standard sizing 3x BB. Will fold to 3bet, call or continue based on board.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5c. Elon on the button has raised to 300. This is a weak, offsuit hand that does not play well out of position against a 3x open. Given the stack sizes and the hand's lack of connectivity or high-card strength, the most profitable long-term play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m in the big blind with 4♠3♣ facing a button open to 300 (I’ve already posted 100, call is 200). 4-3 offsuit is a very weak, poor-playability hand out of position — it performs poorly vs a BTN opening range and will get barreled off most flops. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~30.8% equity, which this hand won’t reliably have, and a min-raise bluff here is too thin given stacks and I’d be OOP post-flop. Preserve chips and fold.

I have been dealt pocket queens (Qh Qd) on the button. This is a premium starting hand. My strategy here is to put in a standard raise to build the pot and narrow the field. Given the blinds of 50/100 and my stack size, a raise to 300 is consistent with previous hands and standard opening sizes.

I have A♣6♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. I already have 50 posted and must call 250 more (total commitment 300). A6o is not strong enough to 3‑bet for value or to polarize — a small 3‑bet to 500 would have limited fold equity and likely put me OOP vs callers or expose me to 4‑bets from Elon. Folding is too tight given the pot odds. Calling is the best line: I’m getting ~2.8:1 on a call (need ~36% equity) and A‑high can realize equity postflop; I can proceed cautiously OOP (continue with top pair/good draws, fold to heavy aggression without improvement).

My hand is 6d4c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. Facing a button raise from Flash (loose tendencies observed in history) and a call from Minni in the SB, the pot is 700 and I need to call 200 more (pot odds ~3.5:1), but this hand has negligible equity against their ranges out of position. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this long 150-hand game, avoiding speculative plays with such a weak holding.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (A♣6♠) on A♠-3♠-4♦ and I'm out of position to the preflop raiser (Flash). Flash's button open is wide, so his range contains many A‑x, broadways, and small pairs — I beat a lot of that but can be crushed by A‑K/A‑Q or by sets (33/44). Betting OOP here builds a pot I'm not comfortable committing to with a 6 kicker: a tiny bet is easily exploited and a larger c‑bet could force a tough decision if he raises. I prefer pot control and information: check. Plan is to call reasonably sized continuation bets (realize equity vs bluffs and worse A‑x), fold to a polarizing huge raise, and if he checks back consider a turn stab or take another free card to re-evaluate.

I have pocket queens on an Ace-high flop after raising pre-flop. While I have a strong pair, the Ace is a scary card as it is squarely in the calling range of the small blind (Minni). However, since Minni checked to me, I will put out a continuation bet of about half the pot. This allows me to gain value from smaller pairs or straight draws (like 56 or 25) while also finding out where I stand. If Minni raises, I will have to consider folding as the Ace likely hit their range.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (A♣6♠) on A♠–3♠–4♦ and I’m out of position. Flash’s button bet to 350 (~1/3 pot) is standard and represents a wide c-bet (many Ax, broadways, small pairs and bluffs) — I’m ahead of a lot of his range but crushed by better A‑x and sets. Pot control and information are the priorities: raising OOP with a 6‑kicker would commit me unnecessarily and force difficult decisions vs further aggression. Calling 350 is cheap (I’m getting roughly 3:1 to call), keeps worse Ax and bluffs in, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now; fold to a large, polarizing raise; call or check‑back vs small turn bets on safe runouts and pursue thin value only on favorable turns.

I have top pair A♣6♠ with a weak kicker on A–3–4–T. Flash is the preflop raiser who c‑bet the flop and I called; his range is wide (many A‑x, broadways, small pairs and bluffs). The turn (T♠) improves my equity slightly (I pick up a one‑card spade flush draw to the river — ~9 outs) but also brings more dangerous spade combinations for him. I want pot control OOP with a marginal but still often‑best hand: betting here would build a pot I don’t want to commit to against better A‑x or sets and would get called by worse A‑x and many floats. Check to keep his bluffs and worse A‑x in, realize equity on the river, and make more informed decisions later. Plan: check; call a reasonable/small river bet to realize equity or fold to large, polarized pressure.

On the turn, the board has an Ace and now a flush-completing Ten of Spades. My pocket Queens are likely behind any Ace in Minni's range, and since she called the flop bet, her range is weighted towards Ax, medium pairs, or draws. The Ten of Spades is a scary card as it completes a potential flush. By checking back, I control the pot size and utilize the showdown value of my Queens without over-committing against a better hand or a completed draw. If she has a weaker pair or was drawing, I'm happy to see a river for free.

I have A♣6♠ and the final board is 4♦–3♠–A♠–T♠–A♥. That gives me trip Aces (Ac + As + Ah) with a 6 kicker. Pot is 1,400 and there is no bet to face. Flash was the button raiser and c‑bet the flop small, I called; both of us checked the turn. His range is wide (many A‑x, broadways, small pairs and spade draws).

What beats me on this river: pocket sets that turned into full houses (33, 44, TT) and two‑spade hole combos that make an ace‑high flush; also Ad+strong kicker can make the same trips but win on kickers. Quads are not possible with me holding Ac. Betting now would either fold out worse hands that I want to see (thin value) or get raised by polarized hands (full houses/strong trips/flushes) that dominate me — so betting gains little and risks a costly raise.

Decision: check and go to showdown. This controls the pot, realizes equity against the many worse hands in his range, and avoids bloating the pot when a raise usually indicates a hand that already beats me. If he leads a small river bet I will call for value; I will fold to a large, clearly polarized raise.

The river brings a second Ace to the board (4d 3s As Ts Ah). My hand is now two pair, Aces and Queens. However, Minni called my flop bet on an Ace-high board, which strongly indicates she has an Ace or a pocket pair. Given that there are two Aces on the board, any Ace in her hand now makes three of a kind. Since she has checked twice (turn and river), she may be in 'check-call' mode with an Ace or a mid-range pair. I have significant showdown value with my Queens, but betting is unlikely to be called by many worse hands, and I would be forced to fold to a check-raise if she is trapping with an Ace. Checking back is the most prudent play to win the pot at showdown.

2♦9♣ is a junk hand — one of the worst offsuit combos. Pot is 150 and I must call 100 (need ~28.6% equity) against a raiser’s typical opening range; 9-2o has far less equity and is dominated. A light 3-bet bluff would be high-variance here (deep stacks, likely callers or strong opening ranges), so the +EV play over the long run is to fold and wait for a better button opportunity.

7d4c is a trash hand: offsuit, low cards, large gap, no straight or flush potential. In SB facing BB only (Minni folded), pot odds are about 3:1 to call 50 into 150, but equity against BB defending range is poor (~35-40% vs tight, less vs loose), and postflop playability OOP is terrible - prone to being dominated by Ax, suited connectors. Opponents' stacks: Flash deep (23k), me medium (9k), no need to gamble. Standard fold to preserve stack for 87 more hands.

94o is a trash hand, offsuit gapped low cards with no potential. On the button facing a small call of 100 into 150 (decent pot odds but still requires ~28% equity), but postflop playability is poor with reverse position risks and low showdown value. Opponents from history are aggressive preflop but this spot is standard fold to preserve stack for premium hands. Deep stacks but no need to speculate with bottom-tier holding. Fold to wait for better opportunities in 3-handed play.

In a blind-vs-blind situation, Queen-Jack suited is a very strong hand. With Elon folding the button, I am opening for a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value against Minni's big blind.

I’m in the big blind with 6♠2♦ facing a small‑blind open to 300 after the button folded. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more — that requires about 200/(400+200)=33% equity. 6‑2o is one of the worst hands: heavily dominated by most of the SB’s opening range, very poor playability out of position, and carries big reverse implied‑odds when I do pair. A light 3‑bet/bluff isn’t realistic here (no blockers, villain is deep and likely to call or shove). With a 74BB stack there’s no need to spew chips in a marginal spot — fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Q8s, which is a solid hand to open in a three-handed game. My stack is very healthy (over 230 BBs), while both of my opponents are sitting around 70-90 BBs. Raising here allows me to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Q8s plays well post-flop due to its flush and straight potential.

Facing a button open to 300 from SB with Ad6s: this is a playable, suited ace that performs well three-handed and has a useful blocker to other aces. Calling (I need to add 250 more to have 300 total in) keeps the pot controlled, preserves SPR with ~73bb stacks, and lets the big blind act — a small min-raise to 500 would be tiny and won’t fold out enough hands, while a larger 3-bet with a marginal hand like A6s is unnecessarily high-variance out of position. I’ll call to see a flop and evaluate — if I hit an Ace or a strong draw I can proceed; if I face heavy aggression preflop or on the flop I can fold without committing too much.

9c4h is a very weak offsuit hand with no connectivity, straight potential, or flush draw possibilities. Facing a button raise from Flash (loose-aggressive tendencies from history) and a call from Minni, entering a multiway pot OOP with this hand offers terrible equity and playability post-flop. Pot odds (700:200 ~3.5:1) do not justify calling given the hand's ~20-25% equity vs typical ranges. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stacked game.

Dry rainbow flop (T♠-4♣-3♥). I hold A♦6♠ — no pair, only backdoor spade and the Ace blocker. Pot is 700 and my stack is ~7100 (SPR ≈ 10). I called preflop to keep the pot controlled and this flop is exactly the sort of texture where I want to preserve that plan: betting as a bluff can win the pot now, but it also bloats the pot out of position and exposes me to a check‑raise or sizeable continuation bet I don’t want to face with essentially zero equity. Against a wide opening range the button will c‑bet frequently, so checking lets him take the lead and gives me information while preserving chips. Plan: check, call smaller c‑bets for showdown value because my Ace blocks a lot of A‑x combos, but fold to large pressure or a check‑raise. If he checks back I get a free card to improve to top pair and can re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have position and the initiative after raising pre-flop with Qc8c. The flop (Ts 4c 3h) is relatively dry and favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor. Although I haven't hit a pair, I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. I will place a continuation bet of approximately half the pot to put pressure on Minni and potentially take the pot down immediately.

Out of position on a dry rainbow flop (T♠-4♣-3♥) with A♦6♠. I checked to keep the pot controlled and induce information — Flash fires a standard-sized c-bet of 350 into the pot. This is a call: my Ace-high has real showdown value (and blocks a lot of A‑x value combos), the bet size gives me ~25% break‑even equity (call 350 to win a ~1400 pot), and I have no compelling bluffing or value-raise reasons here. Raising would be high-variance and thin given my lack of pair or strong draw; folding would be too passive given the pot odds and blocker. Plan: call to see a turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair the Ace or pick up equity, and extract value if I improve.

I check. The turn (J♦) didn't improve me — I still have only A‑high with the Ace blocker. The flop c‑bet and my call were about pot control; betting now as a bluff is thin because Flash’s wide c‑betting range includes many hands that will call (T‑x, J‑x, broadways) or raise, and I don't want to inflate the pot out of position with no pair. Pot is 1400 (SPR ≈4.8), so checking preserves chips, allows me to realize my showdown equity, and keeps options open for the river. Plan: call small river bets with my blocker and folded‑equity considerations, fold to large river aggression unless I pair or see a clear bluff.

The Jack of Diamonds is an excellent card for my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. It's a classic 'scare card' that may have improved me to top pair or better. Furthermore, with my Queen and Eight, I have picked up a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine). By betting again (a 'double barrel'), I put significant pressure on Minni's likely holdings like middle pairs (77-99) or weak Tens, which may now feel forced to fold. I will bet 900 into the 1400 pot to maintain aggression and capitalize on the board texture.

I stick with my earlier plan: this is a fold. Board: 4♣-3♥-T♠-J♦, my hand A♦6♠. I have no pair and essentially no draw (only backdoor sixes/aces — 3 A + 3 6 = ~6 outs → ~13% to improve on the river). The pot is 2,300 and I must call 900, so the break‑even equity required is 900 / (2,300+900) ≈ 28%. My raw equity (~13%) is well below that. Flash’s sizing (c‑bet on the flop, big bet on the turn) is polarized toward value here; while I block some A‑x combos, it’s not enough to justify calling or bluff‑raising out of position. A raise would be a high‑variance bluff that contradicts my preflop/ flop plan and is unlikely to fold out enough of his value range. Preserving stack and folding to this sizable turn aggression is the correct, +EV choice.

I'm on the button with 9♠8♣ in a three-handed pot. Facing a required call of 100 into a 150 pot gives ~40% break-even equity, and this hand plays well in position (straight and two-pair potential + good implied odds with deep stacks). I don't want to 3-bet light here since Flash (BB) is deep and defends often and Elon (SB) has shown aggression — calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves fold equity postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, use position to realize equity; if I miss and face heavy action I will fold, but if I hit a draw or pair I will proceed aggressively to extract value.

Preflop in 3-handed with AKo from SB facing a button limp. AK is a premium hand that dominates limping ranges and wants to raise for value, isolation against the loose button (Minni who limps/raises frequently), and to thin the field before the BB acts. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard sizing here: builds the pot when ahead, applies pressure OOP but hand is strong enough postflop. Folding or limping is too weak; calling the 50 is suboptimal as it misses value and builds pot OOP multiway.

I am in the Big Blind with 87o. Minni limped from the button and Elon raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 500+), 87o is a weak hand to play out of position in a potentially multi-way pot. Minni still has to act after me and could potentially 4-bet or call, making the hand even harder to play. Given my large stack, I'll stick to a more disciplined strategy and fold this speculative hand.

Call to 300. 9♠8♣ is a playable, connected hand in position and with ~66bb effective stacks I have good implied odds to realize straights/two-pair.
