Elon has shown wide preflop and postflop aggression, but I don’t want to 3-bet light and bloat the pot out of position; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position to extract or fold as appropriate. If I hit a pair or a strong draw I can apply pressure; if I miss and face heavy aggression I’ll give it up.

Flop board 8h Tc Jc is very wet and draw-heavy: two clubs for flush draw, connected cards (9 straight draw, Q9 open-ender, etc.). AKo has complete air: ace-high, no pair, no flush draw (Ah but only one heart on board), no straight draw (no gutshot even). Raised preflop OOP with premium AK, Minni called from button with loose range per history (frequent limps/calls/raises). Pot 700 OOP first to act. Betting is poor: thin value unlikely as reps strong hands but gets called/raised by draws/better; bluff equity low OOP vs button calling station tendencies. Best play: check to pot control, induce bluffs/checks from worse, fold to bets (easy vs any bet). If she checks behind, ace-high showdown possible. Maximizes EV long-term by avoiding spew.

Flop: 8h Tc Jc — I hold 9♠8♣: middle pair (pair of eights) plus an open‑ended straight draw (7 or Q completes) — 8 outs (~29% to complete by river) and a small backdoor club possibility. Elon opened preflop and then checked the flop, which often shows weakness or a wide, uncommitted range. In position with a hand that has both current value and substantial equity, I want to take the initiative: a bet will get value from worse pairs, calls from single overcards and draws, and can fold out pure floats and some overcards that currently have ~0 equity vs my combos.

Sizing: I’ll size down to ~1/3 pot (250 into 700). That achieves protection (charges K/Q overcards and some club turn cards), keeps worse hands and draws willing to call, and leaves room to maneuver on the turn. If Elon check‑raises small I can call with my pair+draw; a large raise will force a fold or a very careful decision on the turn depending on sizing. If called and I miss the turn I’ll proceed cautiously (check/call) and pick spots to realize equity or fold to heavy pressure. Betting now follows my preflop plan to be aggressive when I hit a pair/draw and maximizes expected value from this spot.

Facing a 250 bet into 950 pot on a draw-heavy flop (8h Tc Jc) with complete air (AhKd: ace-high, no pair, no draws). Raised pre OOP with AK premium, she called button-wide range. Checked to pot control/induce as planned. Her bet reps value (sets, two-pair, pairs+draws) or strong draws (clubs, open-enders like Q9/KQ/KT/9T). No fold equity for raise OOP vs loose button who calls frequently; calling OOP bloating pot with 0 equity/showdown value, realizes no EV. Instant fold preserves stack, avoids spew on wet board where range disadvantaged. Long-term +EV discipline.

Pocket Queens is an extremely strong hand on the button in a 3-handed situation, with excellent position on both opponents. We want to raise for value, to isolate weaker hands, and build the pot while maintaining initiative. Standard raise size pre-flop is 3x the big blind to 300, which is common sizing observed in previous hands. This puts pressure on the blinds, and our deep-ish stacks (86BB) support it. Limping or calling is too passive with this premium hand.

I am in the Small Blind with KTo. Elon has opened from the Button to 3x the big blind. King-Ten offsuit is a standard defending hand in this position against a Button open. While playing out of position is not ideal, the pot odds are sufficient to justify a call, and the hand has decent high-card value. I will call to see the flop and evaluate further action based on the board texture and Minni's response in the Big Blind.

I’m in the big blind with A♥2♦. Elon opened to 300 on the button and Flash (SB) called, so it’s a 3-way pot of 700 and I must put in 200 more (total 300). A2o is worth defending from the BB versus a button open, but it’s a marginal hand to 3-bet multiway — the minimum raise to 500 is too small to isolate and will likely get called, leaving me OOP with weak kicker and little fold equity. Calling realizes equity, takes advantage of decent pot odds (~22%), and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: call to see the flop; on the flop I will proceed cautiously OOP — fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the Ace or pick up strong draws, otherwise try to realize equity and look for spots to extract value or pot-control.

On the flop of 5s-Ac-Kd, I have middle pair with a Ten kicker. The board is quite dry but features two overcards to the Ten and an Ace and King which likely hit the pre-flop aggressor's (Elon) range. I am first to act. Checking is the standard play here as the pre-flop caller to see how the original raiser reacts. Leading out into two opponents on an Ace-high board where I don't have the Ace is risky and doesn't accomplish much. I will check and evaluate based on my opponents' actions.

I have top pair (A♥) with a very weak kicker (2♦) on A♣ K♦ 5♠ in a 3-way pot. With Flash checked into me and Elon yet to act, I should control the pot OOP — betting thin for value is risky because both opponents can easily have better Ax or Kx hands and there are still many turn cards that can change things. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and re-evaluate based on Elon’s action: call small to medium-sized bets to extract value, but fold to large raises or heavy turn aggression unless I improve.

Pocket Queens remain strong on this Ac Kd 5s flop, which is dry with no flush draws and limited straight possibilities (e.g., no broadway draws completed, just gutshots like 67). As pre-flop raiser, both opponents checked, indicating weakness or traps, but my position and initiative favor a continuation bet for value against top pair hands like AJs, KJo, QJs, or weaker pairs/draws like 76s, A5s. QQ has good showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards, so betting protects it and builds the pot. Half to 2/3 pot sizing (600 into 900) is standard, denies equity to floats/draws, and fits observed bet sizes in prior hands (e.g., 350-900 on similar pots). Stacks allow it (Minni covered, deep SPR), and history shows opponents call light but fold bluffs often.

On a board of 5s-Ac-Kd, I have middle pair with King-Ten. Elon has made a 2/3 pot continuation bet after opening pre-flop. While the Ace is a significant overcard and very much in the pre-flop aggressor's range, folding second pair immediately in a 1,500 chip pot for a 600 chip call might be overly cautious, especially given Elon's frequency of aggression. I will call this bet to see the turn, but I must be wary of further aggression if I don't improve, as I am out of position and an Ace is likely to be in his range.

Call 600. I checked the flop to control the pot and Elon c-bet ~600 (~2/3 pot); Flash called, so this is a 3-way pot. I have top pair (A♥) with a very weak kicker (2♦). Folding here surrenders too much equity; raising OOP multiway is thin (little fold equity and I’ll often get called by better Aces, Kx or pocket pairs), so calling is the prudent line. Pot odds: calling 600 into a 2100 pot (final pot 2700) requires ~22% equity — my one-pair hand is generally worth that versus their likely ranges. Plan: call to keep the pot manageable and re-evaluate on the turn — extract value if I improve, but be ready to fold to heavy turn aggression (or a clean two-pair/two-tone board change) since my kicker is weak.

I have improved to three of a kind (Kings) on the turn. The board is now 5s-Ac-Kd-Kc. Given that Elon was the pre-flop aggressor and followed up with a significant bet on the flop into two opponents, he likely has a strong range that includes many Aces. By checking here, I am allowing Elon to continue his aggression with hands like AJ, AQ, or even a total bluff, while I am currently ahead of almost everything except AA, AK, or 55. Checking also keeps Minni in the pot, potentially allowing her to catch a second-best hand or attempt a bluff. I will check and see how the action develops.

I hold A♥2♦ and the turn (K♣) gives me two pair — Aces and Kings (board: 5♠ A♣ K♦ K♣). In a three‑way pot this is a strong hand, but the turn is a very bad card for me: any K in an opponent’s hand now makes trips and AK/AA/KK convert into full houses/quads. Elon opened pre and c‑bet the flop; both opponents called, so there’s a meaningful chance one of them has a king or a hand that turned into a full house. Raising or betting out OOP in a multiway spot here is thin (very little fold equity and a good chance of being check‑raised/raised off a second‑best hand). I want to control the pot size and get more information from the button. Action: check. Plan: if Elon fires a small turn bet I will usually call to extract value from worse Aces and to see the river; if he puts in large, polarized aggression (a big shove/raise), I will strongly consider folding because that line is likely trips/full house. If both check through we can evaluate the river and decide whether to bet for value or pot‑control again. Overall: pot‑control and avoid getting committed OOP to hands that currently beat me.

The turn pairs the kings on a board of 5s Ac Kd Kc, which is extremely bad for our QQ as it improves any Kx hand (like AK, KQ, KJ, or even pocket kings unlikely but possible) to trips or better, and Ax hands remain ahead. Our preflop raise and flop c-bet got called by two opponents, suggesting they likely have Ax, Kx, or strong pairs/draws that hit the flop reasonably. Both checking the turn again indicates possible weakness (e.g., Ax checking back for pot control, weaker hands), traps, or missed draws, but QQ has poor showdown value here—behind any A or K, and vulnerable to better. Position allows a check to pot control, realize equity for free against bluffs or weaker Ax, and avoid bloating the pot further with SPR low (pot 2700, effective ~6k with Minni). Betting risks getting called by better (thin value from AJ/QJ/55 unlikely given calls) or raised; no strong bluff candidates since board favors value. Opponent tendencies: frequent check-calls but fold to aggression post-turn in history. Check preserves stack for better spots.

On the river, the board is 5s-Ac-Kd-Kc-5h. I have Kings full of Fives, which is a very strong hand. After the turn checked around, the action remains on me first in the Small Blind. Checking here again is a trap strategy to encourage either Minni or Elon to bet a hand like an Ace or a missed draw bluff, as my line looks very much like a weak hand that has given up. Given the relatively shallow stacks remaining (Elon has 7,725 and Minni has 6,250), I want to extract value, but if I lead out, they might fold their marginal hands. If they both check, I win a decent pot; if one of them bets, I can raise to maximize value.

Board is 5♠ A♣ K♦ K♣ 5♥; I hold A♥2♦, so I have two pair (Aces and Kings) with a terrible 2 kicker. This is a very fragile hand in a multiway pot — anyone holding a K or a 5 now has a full house (or trips that become a full house with certain kickers), and any opponent with an Ace plus a higher side card than a 2 will have the same two pair but a better fifth card and thus beat me. Action line: button (Elon) opened, c-bet flop and then checked the turn; SB (Flash) called flop and checked turn. Both checked the river to me, which is consistent with pot‑control or medium strength hands but doesn’t eliminate K/5 combinations that crush me. Betting here is thin: I’d get called by better hands and rarely fold out hands I beat; multiway dynamics make a thin value bet or bluff low EV. Best play is pot‑control and take it to showdown. I check and show down rather than bloating the pot or risking value‑calls from many hands that beat me.

The river pairs the fives on a KK55A board (5s Ac Kd Kc 5h), making the board two pair (kings over fives) with an ace kicker by default. Pocket queens gives us exactly the board strength—no improvement, so we beat only hands worse than the board (e.g., pure air, small pocket pairs under 5s), but lose to any ace (aces full of kings), king (kings full of fives), or five (fives full of kings). Both opponents called our flop c-bet on AcKd5s (dry board), strongly suggesting ranges heavy with Ax (pair aces turning into full house), Kx (top pair becoming full house), or sets/draws that connected. Their turn and river checks could be pot control with monsters, traps, or medium strength, but light calling ranges from history don't include much that chops or loses (e.g., missed draws unlikely to call 600 into 900 pre-turn). Position allows a free showdown; betting risks calls/shoves only from better hands (low fold equity on paired board, value-heavy spot), while thin value from worse is minimal. Checking preserves our ~7k stack (SPR ~3 post-river), realizes equity against unlikely bluffs, and avoids spew in a cooler-heavy spot against opponents who check-call flops but check rivers often when strong. Consistent with turn pot control.

I am on the button with 9h 6c. This is a weak hand and generally a fold in most positions. However, since it's a 3-handed game and I am on the button, I could consider opening to steal the blinds. Looking at the table dynamics, Minni (SB) and Elon (BB) have been relatively active and have played fairly straightforwardly. Given my hand is quite weak and I'm looking to maintain my stack lead rather than taking unnecessary risks with air, a fold is a safe and standard play here.

I’m in the small blind with J♦T♠ (offsuit) heads-up vs the big blind. Pot is 150 and calling costs 50; the hand has reasonable playability (broadway/straight potential) but I’ll be out of position postflop, so I want to seize initiative rather than limp. I’ll raise to 300 (a standard ~3bb open) to apply pressure, get fold equity, and define Elon’s range — this size is big enough to fold out some weak big-blind holdings but keeps worse hands in. Plan: if Elon 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal OOP hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play disciplined postflop. Total committed: 300.

73o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and terrible post-flop playability out of position. Minni's small blind raise after button fold is likely wide, but my hand has minimal equity against her range (~30-35%) and no implied odds with such disconnected low cards. Defending BB this wide leads to difficult spots OOP. Pot odds are ~3:1 but not sufficient given reverse implied odds and Minni's aggression shown in history (frequent raises and continuation bets). Folding preserves stack for better spots in this long session of 150 hands.

I’m on the button with T9o — a playable hand that plays well in position. There’s 150 in the pot and 100 to call; I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to two blinds, and narrow the field while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300 total) to get fold equity vs wide blind defending ranges and to give myself positional advantage postflop. If I face a large 3-bet from Elon or Flash I’ll re-evaluate (usually folding to a big 3-bet), but I’m happy to continue in position against calls.

6c2h is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em: offsuit, low cards, disconnected, no suited potential for flushes. Facing a button raise from Minni, who raises frequently from history, in the small blind out of position. Calling 250 into 450 pot gives poor implied odds even if hit, and playing OOP postflop with this hand is disastrous. No raise makes sense without premium. Preserve stack for better spots in cash game aiming to maximize over 150 hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in poker. Minni has raised from the button to 300. While the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, 7-2 offsuit realizes its equity extremely poorly and I would be out of position for the entire hand. There is no strategic reason to defend this hand or attempt a bluff 3-bet here given the circumstances.

3-handed on the button preflop with 83o. While button opening range is wide (50-70% of hands typically), 83 offsuit is a low, uncoordinated hand with poor playability postflop, dominated by likely calling/raising ranges, and minimal straight/flush potential. Opponents: Flash deep but aggressive historically, Minni ~64BB also aggressive (frequent raises). Pot odds to limp are poor for realization, and limping invites multiway pots with trash. Raising risks variance with weak hand. Standard fold to wait for superior spots to maximize EV over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Jh 3h. Since the Button folded, it's just me and Minni in the Big Blind. While Jh 3h is not a premium hand, being suited gives it some playability. I'll put in a standard raise to 300 to attempt to win the pot pre-flop. If Minni calls, I'll evaluate the flop, though I'll be out of position for the rest of the hand.

I have pocket 4s in the big blind facing a single raise from the small blind to 300. With 100 already posted I need to call 200 (300 total). This is a classic set-mining spot: flopping a set is only ~11–12% (about ~19% by the river) so the immediate pot odds alone aren’t great, but the villain is very deep and opens a lot, so implied odds are strong. I don’t want to bloat a multi-street pot out of position with a small pair by 3‑betting; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity. Plan: call, look to extract if I hit a set, otherwise proceed cautiously to folds versus heavy aggression on scary boards.

I have flopped trips with a Jack (Jh) on a board of Td Jc Jd. This is an extremely strong hand. I am out of position against Minni, who called pre-flop. I want to build a pot, but I also want to keep Minni's range in the hand, which likely includes some Tens, middle pairs, or diamond draws. A check here can induce a bet or allow her to catch up slightly. However, leading out for a small value bet is also standard to get value from draws or weaker pairs. Given the texture, I'll check to see if she bets, planning to call or raise depending on the sizing.

I have pocket 4s on Td Jc Jd — an underpair on a very wet/connected-looking board with paired jacks. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked; this line and the board mean his range contains a lot of Jx/Tx and stronger pairs, so I have very little value and no realistic draw (I only have one diamond and would need both remaining diamonds). Checking is the right play to control the pot and realize equity. If Flash fires a small c-bet I can consider a call to see a cheap turn and hope to hit a 4, but I will fold to sizable pressure — I won’t bluff-raise into a raiser on this board because his range dominates mine. Plan: check, keep pot small, evaluate turn (call small bets, fold to large bets).

I have trips with a Jack, and the turn is the Queen of Clubs. The board is now Td-Jc-Jd-Qc. This board is quite coordinated, but I still have a very strong hand. Since I checked the flop and Minni checked back, her range likely contains some pairs or draws that didn't want to bet immediately. The Queen might have helped her if she has K9, AK, or a Queen, but it also gives me more value from those hands. I'll check again to see if she takes the bait and bets a Queen or a draw, or to control the pot size in case she has exactly K9 or AK.

Board is Td Jc Jd Qc; I have 4d4h — an underpair with essentially no realistic draw (only 2 outs to make a set on the river ≈ 2/46 ≈ 4.3%). Flash was the pre‑flop raiser and has checked flop and turn, so his checked range likely contains a lot of Qx/TX/broadway hands and also some missed floats — but it also contains many hands that currently dominate me (Jx, Tx, Qx, any pocket pair ≥ TT). Betting here as a bluff is thin: a small bet will often be called by Q/T/jack‑type hands and a check‑raise would put me in an unrecoverable spot with virtually no equity. Pot is only 600 versus my 6,200 stack — so I’m not trying to bloat the pot with an underpair. Plan: check and go to the river for a cheap card; only fold to meaningful pressure on the river unless I hit my set (very unlikely).

On a board of Td-Jc-Jd-Qc-Ts, I hold Jh-3h, giving me a full house, Jacks full of Tens (Jh-Jc-Jd-Ts-Td). This is an extremely strong hand, only beaten by the case Jack (Js) for quads or pocket Queens for Queens full of Jacks. Given the action (check-check on flop and turn), it's highly unlikely Minni holds those specific hands. She likely has a Queen, a Ten, or a straight (with a King). I will bet for value, hoping to get called by any of those weaker made hands.

River: Td Jc Jd Qc Ts (board is double‑paired: TT and JJ). I hold 4d4h — my pair is irrelevant: the best 5 cards for me are TT JJ 4, meaning my hole cards only supply a 4 kicker. Flash, the preflop raiser, checks flop/turn and now bets 400 into a 1000 pot (small value/pressure bet). Any hand with a T or J is a full house and crushes me. Any hand with a kicker higher than 4 (A,K,Q,9–5, etc.) also beats me because the board’s two pairs use the kicker as the tiebreaker; Qx additionally beats me and is a plausible thin value bet. Only extremely rare combos (both hole cards ≤3, or exact ties with remaining 4s) are behind me. Pot odds for a call are 1400:400 → ~3.5:1 (≈28.6% equity required). My realistic equity vs Flash’s betting range is far below that (single digits to low teens at best), so a call is +EV negative. Raising as a bluff is catastrophic — I have no meaningful blockers to T/J/Q and will be crushed or raised off by full houses. Preserving 6,200 chips for better opportunities is the correct play.

On the button in a 3-handed game, I have a suited Queen (Q6c), which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play a controlled pot in position. My standard opening size has been 300, so I will continue with that consistency.

I have A♣T♥ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300. ATo is strong enough to defend here but not strong enough to 3-bet out of position (a min-raise to 500 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and a larger 3-bet bloats a pot where I’ll be first to act postflop). I’ll call the extra 250 (300 total committed including my 50 SB) to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and evaluate based on the flop texture and opponents’ reactions. Versus one opponent I’ll play aggressively on good flops; if Elon cold-calls making it a multiway pot I’ll be more cautious and look to realize equity rather than build the pot. If I face a large 3-bet I will fold or proceed very narrowly given ATo’s vulnerability to stronger Ax and broadway hands.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 by Flash and a SB call by Minni (to 300 total). Pot is 700, calling 200 more for excellent pot odds (~3:1 direct, better implied). Hand A3s is a standard defend from BB: suited ace blocker, flush potential, live ace blocker vs AA/AKs, good playability multiway or heads-up postflop where I have position on Minni. Flash's button range is wide, Minni's SB call is loose. Not strong enough to 3-bet (no premium), not weak enough to fold given odds and position. Call and see flop.

Flop is 5♦-9♦-2♥. I have A♣T♥ — no pair, no diamond draw, just Ace-high. This is a multiway pot (button opened, BB called) and I’m out of position to both opponents. C-betting here with a marginal hand is thin: low fold equity multiway and plenty of hands that will continue (pairs, diamonds, big A‑x). I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and re-evaluate based on opponents’ actions on the flop/turn. Plan: call a small probe bet if the price is right, fold to large aggression, and if everyone checks through I’ll go to showdown or look for a better turn spot to apply pressure if I pick up equity.

Flop completely misses my A3o (suited preflop but irrelevant now). No pair, no flush draw (hearts or diamonds), no straight draw (3 doesn't connect meaningfully). Pure ace-high with poor kicker. Board: 5d9d2h is somewhat drawy (diamond flush draw, gutshots like T8/67), favoring aggressive ranges. Preflop: multiway pot after button wide raise and loose SB call. Minni's check suggests weakness, but betting thin into two opponents with 25bb effective stacks (Minni shorter at ~59bb? Wait, 5900/100=59bb) is low EV – too many better Ax/DX/PX in ranges that call or raise. Check to pot control, induce bluffs from Flash's wide range or take free cards. Fold to significant aggression; reassess turn with position over Minni and Flash behind. Preflop call was +EV for multiway implied odds, but now check-fold equity dominates.

I opened pre-flop from the button with Qc6c and both blinds called. The flop is 5d9d2h, which is a very dry board and mostly misses my range but also likely misses a lot of their calling ranges. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and it checked to me, a continuation bet is standard here to try and take the pot down immediately. A bet of around half the pot should be enough to fold out random overcards that didn't hit a pair or a diamond draw. If I am called, I have backdoors to a straight and flush and overcards.

Flop: 5♦-9♦-2♥; I hold A♣T♥. I checked to control the pot and see how opponents react. Flash (BTN) leads for 450 into a 900 pot (pot now 1350) — a half‑pot probe. Folding is too tight given the price; calling 450 to win 1800 requires ~25% equity. ATo has reasonable showdown value here and blocks some strong A‑x/broadway combos. Raising as a bluff OOP into the bettor (and with Elon still to act behind me) is thin and likely to get called or raised by better hands. Plan: call the 450 to see a turn, realize equity, and fold to significant raise or continued large aggression; if I hit an Ace/Ten or the action checks through I’ll re‑evaluate for value on later streets.

Facing a half-pot bet from Flash on a drawy flop (diamond flush draw, gutshots possible) after Minni's check-call. My Ah3s is pure ace-high with no pair, no draws, and a weak kicker. Preflop call was standard for pot odds and position, but flop misses completely. Multiway now after Minni's call, which likely means she has some equity or strength (pair or draw). Flash's button range is wide but betting into two, and Minni calling thins it. Pot odds are ~4:1 (450 to call into 1800), but equity realization is low multiway: little showdown value, poor reverse implied odds if ace hits turn/river (outkicked likely). Position on Minni is nice, but Flash acts last post-turn. Best to fold as planned in flop thoughts: check-fold equity dominates vs aggression. Preserve stack for better spots; no need to continue with bluffcatcher in tough multiway pot on dangerous board.

I hold A♣T♥ on 5♦-9♦-2♥-9♣. After calling Flash’s half‑pot probe on the flop the turn pairs the 9 and I’m out of position against a single opponent who opened preflop and c‑bet the flop. Betting OOP here as a bluff has very little fold equity (button has many calling/value hands and plenty of 9x combinations that now improved), and leading into a deep stack risks getting raised off a marginal hand. My Ace‑high has decent showdown value and blocks many strong A‑x/value combos, so the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and control the pot. If Flash bets a small-to-medium amount I will often call to realize equity against a wide continuing range; if he barrels big I’ll fold given the board texture and my lack of improvement. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river for a thin value bet or showdown.

The turn card is the nine of clubs, pairing the top card on the board. After being called on the flop, checking back here is a prudent play for pot control. While the nine is a card I could certainly represent as part of my range, Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of this board (like a pair of fives, a small pocket pair, or a diamond draw). By checking, I see a free river card and keep the pot small, which is beneficial since I currently only have Queen-high. This also allows me to potentially catch a bluff on the river or hit one of my overcards.

Board is 5♦-9♦-2♥-9♣-8♠ and I hold A♣T♥. Flash led flop for 450 and I called; he checked the turn. That line (c-bet then check) heavily weights toward missed c-bets, overcards, small pairs and busted diamond draws rather than strong 9x/sets (those would more often bet for value on the turn). My Ace-high has decent showdown value and also blocks some of Flash’s stronger Ace combos. Betting OOP here as a bluff is thin — I have limited fold equity vs a check‑turn range that will often call a river bluff, and a thin value bet will just get called by better hands. The lowest-variance, highest-EV line is to check back and take my showdown. If Flash now barrels a small bet I’ll call for pot odds; I’ll fold to a large polarizing shove. For now: check and go to showdown.

The board is 5d-9d-2h-9c-8s. I have Queen-high (Qc6c). I bet the flop and was called by Minni, then we both checked the turn. Minni has now checked the river. While my Queen-high has almost no showdown value and will lose to any pair or any Ace-high, Minni's check-call on the flop followed by two checks suggests she has a marginal hand like a small pair (5x, 2x) or a missed draw. While a bluff could potentially force a fold from a weak 5 or a missed diamond draw, Minni has shown tendency to be sticky in previous hands. Given the pot size and the texture, I will check behind and take the small chance that my Queen-high is good against a lower missed draw, rather than risking more chips on a bluff against a player who might not fold a pair.

I’m on the button 3-handed with J♥7♥ — a marginal but playable, suited hand with good position. In 3-handed play you should open a wide range; raising for fold equity and initiative is +EV. A standard 3x BB open (to 300 total) is small relative to our stacks (keeps pressure, reduces multiway action) and lets me play postflop in position. Plan: c-bet many dry flops, realize equity on draws; fold to a large 3-bet (J7s is not a 3-bet hand), consider calling small 3-bets or calls depending on stack/players. Therefore open-raise to 300.

Q4o is a weak offsuit hand with no connectivity or suited potential. Facing a button raise to 3x from Minni, who is aggressive but this hand has no playability OOP as SB. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 (~36% implied) are poor for a speculative hand with dominated equity. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. My hand is relatively weak but does contain an Ace, and I am getting decent odds to call at 200 more into a pot that will be 650. However, out of position against Minni, A2o plays poorly. Looking at the stack sizes, Minni has about 70 big blinds. My goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands and minimize unnecessary variance with marginal holdings out of position. While a call is justifiable, I will choose to fold this time to wait for a better spot, as A2o is easily dominated and hard to play post-flop.

3-handed pre-flop on the button with Js2d, a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no suitedness, and poor high-card strength. Acting first pre-flop, standard play is to fold weak hands like this to avoid playing out of position post-flop with a dominated hand. Pot odds to limp are irrelevant given the hand's lack of equity against calling ranges. Preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. The Button has folded, so it is just me and Minni in the Big Blind. K5o is a standard opening hand in a blind-versus-blind situation to attempt a steal or play with initiative. Minni has shown some aggression by 3-betting my opens in the past, but I will continue with a standard raise to 300 to maintain pressure and potentially win the pot immediately.

I have K♦Q♣ in the big blind facing a small‑blind open to 300 (pot 400, I must call 200). Heads‑up this is a very strong hand against a wide SB opening range. I want to apply pressure, isolate the opener and get some fold equity rather than passively seeing a flop out of position. A standard 3‑bet size to about 900 (total) accomplishes that — big enough to narrow ranges and discourage speculative calls, yet small relative to my 7.3k stack so I don't overcommit. Plan: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls we proceed to the flop with a strong hand and some initiative; if he 4‑bets big I will re‑evaluate and likely fold KQo to a large 4‑bet.

I opened K5o from the Small Blind, which is a standard steal attempt. However, Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 900. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call, K5o performs poorly against a typical 3-betting range, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. Minni has shown a tendency to 3-bet with strong hands (like AQ in Hand #50), and I don't want to commit more chips with a dominated hand. Folding is the best long-term play here.

I am on the button with J6o. While this is a weak hand, it is a standard opening candidate in some loose strategies, but in a 3-handed game with a 150-chip pot and blinds of 50/100, the pot odds to call are okay, but raising is generally better if I want to play. However, given the stacks and the relatively weak nature of J6o, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid post-flop complications out of position or against potentially stronger ranges from the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with 2♦5♥ — one of the worst starting hands. The button folded so it’s heads-up vs the big blind (Elon) and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Calling 50 to see a flop with such a poorly dominated, unplayable hand is negative EV, and raising to try to steal is unwarranted against an opponent who defends and applies pressure. Preserve my stack for better spots — fold and surrender the small blind.

I’m on the button with 8♦5♦ (8h5d), pot 150 and a 100 call. 8-5 offsuit is a weak hand, but being on the button against only the blinds gives me positional advantage and fold equity. Calling here would cost 100 to win a 250 pot (roughly 40% break-even equity) which this hand doesn't have. Folding is too tight from the button when I can attempt a steal. I’ll open to the minimum raise (200) — a standard 2xBB size — to apply pressure on the blinds while keeping the size small so I can fold to significant resistance. If I face a 3-bet, I'll let it go; if called, I’ll play the flop in position and proceed cautiously with a marginal holding.

Pocket threes is a strong set-mining hand with excellent implied odds given the effective stacks around 7k and the small raise size from the button. Minni's button raise is likely wide based on her frequent opening strategy observed in prior hands (e.g., many small raises from late position). Pot odds are favorable: calling 150 more into a 350 pot (23% immediate odds), but sets hit ~12% and pay off big OOP against her range. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks her wide 4-bet range while I'm OOP. Calling keeps the pot small, disguises strength, and allows BB to act behind. Will play cautiously postflop, folding to heavy aggression unless set or improved.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace of Spades and Queen of Diamonds (AQo), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni raised to 2BB from the button and Elon called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 500, and it costs me only 100 to call. However, AQo is a premier hand that performs well with a raise to thin the field or extract value, especially since both opponents have relatively short stacks (74.5BB and 70.75BB). A 3-bet here is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially end the hand immediately. I will raise to 800.

I opened to 200 from the button with 8♥5♦ as a steal attempt, but Flash in the big blind 3-bet to 800 and Elon (SB) has already called. Calling would cost me 600 more into a ~1200 pot (roughly 33% break-even equity) and leave me in a multiway pot with a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly postflop. 8-5o has very little equity vs a typical 3-bet range and I have limited fold equity if I 4-bet. Preserving my 7.45k stack and waiting for a better spot is the correct play here — I fold.

Pocket threes set-mined profitably pre-3-bet vs button open, but Flash's BB squeeze after SB call and button fold indicates a strong polarizing range: premiums like QQ+, AK, or bluffs with blockers/suited aces suited connectors. Now OOP heads-up vs deep BB aggressor with 24k stack, calling 600 more into 1200 pot (~33% odds) requires ~30% equity vs his range, but 33 has ~12% set equity + some straight/draw outs, totaling ~20-25% at best vs tight range. Implied odds reduced OOP facing likely c-bet aggression on dry boards, and my 7k effective stack risks large postflop overbets. Already in 200 (3% of stack), but folding preserves stack for dominant spots over 150 hands. 3-betting OOP risks walk/fold equity loss or cooler. Standard fold to squeeze.

3-handed on the button with 8s5s suited connectors. Excellent position for post-flop playability and implied odds. Pot is 150 with 100 to call, offering good odds even if called. Opponents: Flash (SB) deep-stacked and aggressive but folds often; Minni (BB) competent but stack similar to mine. Raising small to 300 total attempts to steal blinds frequently in 3-handed play or take initiative if called. Folding too tight preflop; calling risks multiway pot OOP for blinds. Raise builds stack EV+ over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 4d, which is a very weak hand. Elon (the Button) has raised to 300. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

I’m in the big blind with K♦8♥ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 200 to me). With deep stacks (~70+ BB) and a wide BTN opening range heads-up-ish, K8o is marginal but worth defending. Calling 200 (300 total) gives ~3.25:1 immediate pot odds and lets me realize equity and leverage implied odds postflop. A tiny raise to 500 wouldn’t credibly fold many BTN opens and a larger 3-bet is too ambitious for this holding. Plan: call, see the flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy aggression when I miss, continue when I hit top pair or get good draw equity.

I missed this flop with K♦8♥ — no pair, no legitimate draw (only one diamond between my hand and the board so a flush is impossible). Against a button opener's wide raising range I expected many A-x and T-x combos here. With nothing to extract and being out of position, I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a weak holding. Checking lets Elon c-bet so I can fold to standard/large continuation bets and only invest further vs a small probe I can reasonably float. Plan: check now. If Elon bets small (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 200–225) I will consider calling to realize equity and possibly float; if he fires a standard half-pot or bigger (≈325+), I will fold. I will only raise/lead with a real made hand or a strong draw.

Flop is Ac Th 2d, a somewhat dry board with an ace high card, straight draw possibilities (e.g., KJ, QJ), and a diamond flush draw. My 85s has no pair, no flush draw (spades miss), and only speculative gutshot straight draw potential (needing 3/4/6/7, unlikely). Pure air, but I'm the preflop raiser in position against BB who called wide from out of position and checked, indicating weakness. Pot is 650, my stack 6775 effective vs Minni's 7150. Standard continuation bet to deny equity and take down the pot frequently against a likely weak range. Sizing 350 (~50% pot) balances value (if I hit implied preflop) and bluff frequency in 3-handed dynamic. History shows Minni folds to c-bets often when checked (e.g., Hands 13,15,28). Protects stack long-term by stealing pots aggressively on favorable boards.

Fold. I have K♦8♥ on A♣ T♥ 2♦ — no pair and no realistic draw (I’m not on a flush draw and only have a remote double-card straight hope). Elon opened pre and now bets 350 into a 1000 pot; calling 350 to win ~1350 requires ≈26% equity. Versus a reasonable BTN c‑bet range (lots of A‑x, T‑x and connected/overcard combos after a raise) my king‑high is well below that threshold, and I’m out of position so floating is expensive. Raising would be a pure bluff with little fold equity and too risky OOP. Stick to plan and fold to this sizeable continuation bet.

I am on the button with Ks 7s, a strong suited king. Both of my opponents are relatively short-stacked (Minni with 7100, Elon with 7325). Raising here to put pressure on the blinds is the standard play. A 3x open is consistent with the table dynamics seen so far.

6♥5♥ in the small blind vs a button open to 300 (I already posted 50, so I must call 250). Deep stacks (~70BB) make suited connectors playable for implied odds. Out of position vs the button, 6-5s is not strong enough to 3-bet profitably — a min-reraise to 500 would commit chips with little fold equity and put me in awkward spots. Calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity and exploit postflop when I hit straights/flushes. Plan: call to see the flop (300 total committed). Continue with pairs/strong draws; fold to large aggression when I miss.

73o is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight draw potential, or suitedness. Facing a button raise from Flash, who raises frequently, and a call from Minni in the small blind, the pot is 700 and calling 200 gives poor implied odds for this hand. My equity is minimal against their ranges, even if wide. Standard fold from the big blind to preserve stack for better spots.

I have 6♥5♥ on 8♥ 9♦ J♠ — no pair, only a one-card straight draw to any 7 (4 outs) and a very small runner‑runner heart flush draw. I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Best line is pot control: check and see what the button does. If they c‑bet small I’ll call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; if they fire a large bet/raise I’ll fold. If they check back I get a free turn to continue when I pick up a 7, a heart, or a pair.

On the flop of 8h-9d-Js, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and a backdoor spade draw with my King-high. This board heavily connects with Minni's calling range from the small blind, which includes many middling connectors, pairs, and broadway cards. Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I have the option to check back or fire a continuation bet. Given the texture is very coordinated and favors a defender's range, and I only have King-high with a thin draw, I prefer checking back to see a free turn card. This controls the pot size and allows me to realize my equity without being forced to fold to a check-raise.

On the turn (8♥ 9♦ J♠ 8♦) holding 6♥5♥ I have no pair and only a one-card straight draw to a 7 (4 outs, ~8.7% to hit the river). A heart flush is impossible on the river and pairing a 5 or 6 is a very weak hand that will lose to most of Flash’s likely calling/checked range. Flash checked flop and checked the turn — heads‑up that’s often weakness or a slowplay, but with such marginal equity and out of position I don’t want to bloat the pot by bluffing here. Best line is pot control: check and see the river. Plan: if I make the straight on the river I can bet for value; if the river blanks and Flash bets large I’ll fold, and I’ll only call smaller bets when pot odds/read justify it.

The board is 8h-9d-Js-8d. I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). Minni has checked for the second time. Given the highly coordinated nature of the board, it is very likely she has some piece of it or a better draw. I checked back the flop to realize my equity and keep the pot small. Checking the turn allows me to see a free river card to potentially hit my straight or a King, which might be the best hand. Betting here risks a check-raise from hands like trip eights or a sticky Jack, which would force me to fold my equity.

I missed — 6♥5♥ has no pair and only runner‑runner backdoor prospects. The river completed a very scary board: 8♥ 9♦ J♠ 8♦ 8♠. Trips on board mean any J or 9 in Flash’s calling/raising range becomes a full house and any 8 in hand is quads. Flash checked flop and turn, which can be either weakness or a slowplay; given how strongly the raiser’s range connects with this board (Jx, 9x, pocket pairs, occasional 8x), a river bluff is high risk and easily crushed or raised. Pot is small (~700) relative to both stacks, so there’s no need to force a bluff. I take the low-variance, +EV line: check and fold to meaningful aggression — only consider calling a very small bet with exact pot odds, otherwise let the hands that beat me take it.

On the river, the board is 8h-9d-Js-8d-8s, meaning there are trip eights on the board. My best five-card hand is 8-8-8-J-K (the three eights, the Jack on the board, and my King kicker). This hand beats any pocket pair from 22 to 77 (which would play the board's 8-8-8-J-9) and any Queen-high. However, I lose to any Ace-high, any Jack (Full House J-J-J-8-8), and any Nine (Full House 9-9-9-8-8). Given that Minni has checked three times, it's possible she has a weak hand or is hoping to get to a cheap showdown. There is little value in betting as better hands (Ace-high or better) are unlikely to fold in this small pot, and worse hands will simply fold. I will check back and take my King-high to showdown.

Button vs two blinds with Tc5d is marginal but playable — I have position on both opponents and can open a wide stealing range in 3-handed play. Standard sizing at this table has been ~3x BB, and a raise to 300 gives good fold equity against the blinds while keeping the pot manageable with 68bb effective stacks. I will open-raise to 300 to take the initiative; plan is to fold to a large 3-bet (I have poor equity vs a strong 3-betting range) but continue postflop with top pair, good draws, or decent flop textures and use position to exploit weaker responses.

K7o is a marginal offsuit king-high hand from the small blind facing a button raise in a 3-handed game. Pot odds are about 1.8:1 (250 to call into 450), but the hand plays poorly out of position post-flop, with low connectivity, no flush potential, and frequent domination by better Kx. Button range (Minni) is wide but strong enough to defend. 3-betting has fold equity, but K7o lacks ideal blockers and isn't a premium bluff candidate; min-raise to 500 is too small for value/isolation. BB (Flash) still to act and deep-stacked, could squeeze. Best to fold and wait for stronger hands to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands, preserving stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 2c. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Elon folded the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot of 450. While I am getting decent pot odds (roughly 2.25 to 1), 92s is a very weak hand to play out of position against a button open. Given the current stack sizes and the 3x open size, I prefer to fold and wait for a more profitable spot rather than defending such a marginal hand.

3-handed on the button with T7o in an unraised pot (pot 150, to call 100, likely SB folded and BB checked). Position is excellent, and 3-handed ranges are wide. T7o has decent playability with straight potential and dominates worse tens/sevens. Raising to steal blinds or build pot in position has high EV due to fold equity against BB and great postflop position. Standard open size of 3BB (300).

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 3h, which is a very weak hand. Elon, on the button, has raised to 300. Defending such a weak hand out of position is not profitable, especially when I have a significant chip lead and don't need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Elon opened to 300 from the button (heads-up; Flash folded). I have Kc4c in the big blind — a marginal but suited hand with decent equity against a wide button opening range. The pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total), so my break-even is ~30.8%. Versus a typical wide button range K4s should have roughly that equity or better and also has backdoor flush/straight potential. 3-betting as a light bluff is an option, but out of position with a weak kicker and against an aggressive opponent who often continues, a small 3-bet will be called too often and a large 3-bet bloats the pot with a mediocre hand. I’ll take the flop cheaply, plan to proceed on favorable textures (top pair, strong draws, or good two-way boards) and fold to heavy aggression when I miss. Action: call to see the flop (300 total).

Check. I have middle pair (4s) with a decent kicker but no hearts on a very wet 4♥‑2♥‑9♥ board. Betting out OOP with a marginal pair is thin here — I’ll either get raised off my equity or be forced to pay to protect against lots of flush draws. Checking controls the pot, lets Elon bluff with heart floats and weak overcards, and keeps my calling options versus small/medium c‑bets. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (rough guideline ≤ ~300 into the 650 pot) to realize equity, fold to large overbets or check-raises, and if he checks back re-evaluate the turn (bet for value on safe non-heart/king bricks, proceed cautiously if a heart or a higher pair/overcard hits).

Flop is 4h-2h-9h, monotone hearts. I have Tc7h, giving me the nut flush draw (9 outs) with about 35% equity against most calling ranges. Preflop raiser on a scary board, Minni checked BB after calling raise, showing potential weakness. Position allows me to c-bet for value from weaker draws/pairs, protection, and fold equity against air. Half-pot bet sizes well here to build pot while keeping her in with worse equity. Pot 650, bet 350 (roughly half). High EV play.

I have K♣4♣ — a made pair of fours with a King kicker on a three‑heart flop (4♥ 2♥ 9♥). Elon c‑bets 350 into the ~650 pot (pot now 1000); calling 350 to win 1350 gives me ~26% break‑even equity. Versus a wide button opening/c‑betting range my pair has enough showdown value to continue, and the price is correct. Raising OOP is unattractive — it bloats the pot vs many heart/overcard holdings and will get called by better hands; folding is too weak given the pot odds. I’ll call to keep his bluffs and weaker hands in, realize equity, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to a big barrel or a heart that completes obvious flushes; extract value on safe bricks).

Turn (4♦) gives me trips with K♣4♣ on 4♥‑2♥‑9♥‑4♦. Elon opened wide and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn — his range now contains lots of missed hearts, single overcards and medium pairs, with only a small subset beating me (pocket 2s or 9s for full house, or a two‑heart hand for a flush). Quads are impossible because I hold one of the fours. I’m usually ahead and should extract value and deny a free river that could pair the board again. A medium value bet (~½ pot) will get called by worse hands (Ax/Kx, single‑heart holdings, many one‑pair hands) while not overcommitting if he check‑raises. I’ll bet a size that builds the pot and charges the part of his range that continues — bet 700 chips.

Facing a half-pot turn bet of 700 into 1350 from Minni after she check-called my flop c-bet on a highly coordinated flop (4h-2h-9h). My Tc7h gives me a strong 9-high flush draw (9 clean outs to the flush, ~19% equity to improve by the river). The turn 4d pairs the bottom card but doesn't complete many additional draws. Pot odds are favorable: calling 700 to win 2050 + 700 = 2750 (~3.9:1, needing ~20% equity), which my draw meets or exceeds when considering implied odds if I hit (her stack ~4950 behind). Her range for check-calling flop includes weaker pairs, draws, floats, and some made flushes; the turn lead could be for value (e.g., 4x, sets, two-pair), thin value, or semi-bluff, but fold equity on a raise is uncertain given her stack commitment and history of sticking around with medium strength. Calling maintains pot control in position (acting last on river), preserves stack for future hands, and allows value extraction if the flush hits (river shove possible). Raising risks bloating the pot OOP if she shoves a better hand, while folding is -EV given the odds. Standard play with a premium draw heads-up.

I have K♣4♣ — I flatted the flop, then led the turn for 700 and Elon called. Turn action indicated a range that includes single-heart holdings, medium pairs (9s/2s), and some stubborn overcards. The river 6♥ makes the board 4♥‑2♥‑9♥‑4♦‑6♥ — there are now four hearts on board, so any single heart in Elon’s hand becomes a made flush, and river pairing 6 gives him additional set/full‑house/monster possibilities (pocket 6s, pockets of 2s/9s were already strong). My trips are strong but extremely vulnerable to these completed hands. Betting river for value is thin — most hands that will call a river bet are the ones that beat me (flushes/full houses); there are few worse hands that reliably call. Opt to control the pot and go to showdown: check. If Elon bets a small-to-medium size I’ll call for showdown; I’ll fold to a large raise/overbet that represents a nutted/fullhouse/straight‑line shove.

River 6h completes the heart flush, giving me the 9h-7h-6h-4h-2h flush, a very strong hand likely best or near-nuts heads-up. Minni's line: check-call flop c-bet on monotone draw-heavy board (suggests pairs, weaker draws, floats), donk turn 700 into 1350 after blank 4d (possible value with 44/94/two-pair, or semi-bluff/protection), now checks river after flush completes. This checks back weaker flushes, two-pair, or missed draws, but can call/pot with better. Value bet extracts from her worse made hands (lower flushes if any, top pair, two-pair, sets that don't want action). Position allows control. Pot 2750, bet 1500 (~55% pot) gets value from calling range without overbetting vs possible monsters. Maximizes EV vs her sticky history (e.g., Hand 17,19 where she calls down medium strength).

I have K♣4♣ — trips on the river (4♣ + 4♥ + 4♦) but the 6♥ completes a four‑heart board so any heart in Elon’s hand now beats me, and pocket 2s/9s/6s are full‑house combos that also crush me. Line read: Elon opened wide preflop, small c‑bet on the flop and then called my 700 turn bet — his continuing range includes made flushes (two hearts), single‑heart draws/overs that missed, and medium pairs. His river sizing (1,500 into a 4,250 pot, ≈35% pot) is small and consistent with both thin value and bluffs. Calling 1,500 to win ~5,750 requires ~26% equity; trips have >26% equity vs a wide continuing range and beat enough of his hands to justify a call. Raising is unattractive — it will fold the marginal calls and only get action from hands that already beat me. I’ll call the 1,500 and go to showdown; if he instead makes a larger raise (min‑raise/shove) I’d re‑fold given the strength that line represents.

I am on the button with 8h 6c, a weak hand. While the button is a good position to steal blinds, 8-6 offsuit is near the bottom of my opening range. Minni, in the small blind, has a relatively short stack (41 BBs) and has shown aggression in previous hands, making her more likely to defend or jam over an opening raise. Elon in the big blind also has a manageable stack. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to take marginal risks with sub-par hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up in the SB with Kc Qh — a strong, playable hand. I have post-flop position (in heads-up the SB acts last after the flop), so I want to take the initiative, isolate the BB and build the pot with a hand that plays well multi-street. A standard 3xBB raise to 300 is appropriate: it provides fold equity, keeps weaker hands from seeing a cheap flop, and leaves ~3.8k behind for postflop play. If Elon 3-bets big I’ll reevaluate (fold or call depending on sizing and reads); calling the 50 to check the flop would concede initiative unnecessarily.

Q5o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with no strong coordination potential. Facing a standard 3x raise from the SB (Minni, who raises frequently but likely has a decent range here), defending out of position would be -EV long-term. Pot odds are ~2:1 (400 pot, 200 to call), but the hand has poor post-flop playability, low equity against a typical SB raising range (often dominated by Qx, K5+, suited connectors, pairs), and I'll be OOP. Folding preserves my deep stack (100BB+) for better opportunities in a multi-way game with aggressive opponents. Standard BB defense doesn't include this hand.

I’m on the button with A♥4♠ and 4,250 (≈42.5 BB). Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. This is a clear spot to take the initiative: A4o has blocker value to Ax hands and plays well in position. I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size (300 total) rather than the tiny 2x min-raise — 300 gives meaningful fold equity, reduces multiway callers, and keeps the SPR manageable with my mid-short stack against much deeper opponents. Plan: if both fold I pick up the blinds; if called I will continuation-bet favorable A-high or dry boards and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated or draw-heavy boards. If I face a large 3-bet that commits a big chunk of my stack I’ll fold A4o; I may call small-ish 3-bets to keep the pot controlled, but won’t bloat the pot out of position without a stronger ace or a pair. Raise to 300 to leverage position and fold equity.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). 8c3c is a weak suited connector, unlikely to flop strong hands without improvement, and playing OOP postflop is disastrous with such a marginal hand. Minni raises frequently from late position, likely wide range, but 83s has poor playability and equity realization OOP. BB still to act and can squeeze. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding K8o. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Elon has folded. The pot is currently 450, and it costs me 200 to call, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.25:1. In a 3-handed game, K8o is a standard hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. Minni's stack is relatively short (3,950), so I'll just call to see a flop rather than reraising and potentially bloat the pot out of position.

On a flop of 4-7-6, I have King-high with an overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). This board is quite connected and likely favors the range of the pre-flop caller or the button raiser's lower-end connectors. Being out of position in the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard and most prudent play. I will see how she reacts to this texture before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

I raised preflop and hit top pair (A♠4♠) on a relatively dry but mildly connected flop (4♦ 7♠ 6♣). Flash checked, so I should take the initiative: this hand is too good to check back — I want to extract value from worse pairs/ace-x hands and deny free cards to backdoor straight combos (cards like 3 or 5 can improve many draws). I’ll make a solid but not bloating bet (~half pot) to get value while keeping the pot manageable vs a possible check‑raise from a deeper-stacked opponent. Bet 300 (~46% of the 650 pot) leaves plenty of room to fold to an over-aggressive check-raise but charges draws and worse hands that will call. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (continue on safe cards, proceed cautiously on coordinated cards); if check‑raised big I’ll respect the strength and likely fold unless the raise is small enough to justify a call.

On this flop of 4-7-6, I have King-high with an overcard and a gutshot straight draw. Minni's bet of 300 into a 650 pot offers me excellent pot odds of approximately 3.16:1. Given that I only need about 24% equity to continue and I have roughly 14-16% equity just from my outs to a straight and top pair (not counting the possibility that King-high is currently the best hand), a call is justified. I will see if the turn improves my hand or provides a better opportunity to take the pot.

On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds is added to the 4-7-6 rainbow board. I still only have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). I am out of position against Minni, who has been the aggressor in this hand. Checking is the standard and most strategically sound move here to see how she responds to the Jack before deciding whether to continue with my draw.

I’m on the button with top pair (A4) and the villain (Flash) just check‑called my flop c‑bet. The turn J♦ is a concern only insofar as it gives Jx hands a pair and a reason to check‑raise, but it also brings two diamonds on board so there are limited flush draws (villain would need two diamonds in hand). I want to extract value from worse pairs (6x/7x/pocket pairs), ace‑x, and charge any backdoor draws, while keeping the pot manageable versus a possible check‑raise. A half‑pot value/protection bet is the right balance here: large enough to deny correct odds to many draws and get called by worse, but small enough that I can fold to a strong, large check‑raise. Bet 600 into the 1250 pot. If called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and pot‑control or value‑bet depending on the river texture; if Flash check‑raises big I’ll concede and fold unless it’s a very small raise I can call.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and a gutshot straight draw on a board of 4-7-6-J. Minni, who raised pre-flop and bet the flop, has now fired a second barrel of 600 into a pot of 1,250. This gives me pot odds of approximately 3:1, requiring about 25% equity to continue. With only 4 outs to a straight and potentially 3 outs for a King (which might not even be good if Minni has a Jack or better), my current equity is likely around 14-16%. Given the board's connectivity and Minni's aggression, it is highly probable she has at least a pair or a strong draw that she won't fold. Since I lack the required equity and am out of position against an opponent committed to the pot, the most disciplined play is to fold.

3-handed on the button with excellent position after SB limp and BB check. 69o is playable in this spot due to position and potential for straight draws postflop. Raising to take initiative, potentially isolate the weaker BB range (Minni has been calling light), and steal the pot preflop. Minni's stack is ~48BB, Flash deep but limped weakly. Standard sizing 3x the BB call. Folding too tight, calling allows multiway pot OOP disadvantage.

I am holding AQo in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Elon opened from the Button, likely with a wide range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take the initiative, put pressure on the Big Blind, and build a pot with a hand that is well ahead of a standard Button opening range.

I’m on the BB with J♠7♥. Elon opened BTN to 300 and Flash 3-bet the SB to 1000. Pot is 1400 and it costs me 900 to call (1000 total) — min-raise is 1700. J7o is a weak, often-dominated hand vs a typical SB 3-bet range (pairs, broadways, Axs, some suited connectors), so my equity heads-up is well below the ~28.6% break-even pot odds. I’m out of position, only ~48bb effective, and implied odds are limited, so calling or 4-betting as a bluff is not +EV. Fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 (needing 700 to call) from Flash in the SB after my button open with 6s9c offsuit. Pot 1400 offers ~2:1 odds, and I have position heads-up postflop. However, 69o is a marginal speculative hand lacking suitedness, strong blockers, or nut potential. SB 3-bet ranges over button opens are typically strong (JJ+, AKo, AQs+, some bluffs), where 69o has ~30-35% equity at best and plays poorly OOP if behind. Flash has been aggressive but this sizing suggests polarization. Calling risks difficult postflop spots multiway originally but now HU; still, folding preserves stack (~97BB deep) for superior positions. Standard fold to avoid spew and exploit position with better holdings.

I am on the button with J3o. This is a very weak hand, even in a three-handed game. While button ranges are generally wide, J3o lacks playability and is well below the threshold for a standard open. Given my substantial chip lead and the fact that I want to minimize high-variance spots with junk, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up against Elon in the big blind with A♣7♠. This is a strong enough hand in this spot to open for value and initiative rather than limp-call out of position. I want fold equity and to define his range, so I’ll open-raise to a standard size (3BB) — big enough to discourage very wide calls but not commit too much of my ~47.5BB stack. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll continue with a c-bet on many flops that contain an ace or favourable texture and proceed cautiously if I miss and face heavy aggression. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (A7o is often a fold vs a polar 3-bet OOP), but my default preflop plan is to raise here to build equity and pressure the big blind.

Preflop in BB facing a standard 3x raise to 300 from Minni in SB after Flash folds. Pot is 400, calling 200 more for ~2:1 odds into 600 pot post-call. A8s is a strong defending hand vs SB open: suited ace-high with flush and straight potential, plays well multiway or heads-up OOP. Minni has been aggressive in previous hands, often raising light from early positions including SB, and folds to resistance sometimes (e.g., Hand 13, 14, etc.). Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity postflop where we can hit strong draws or top pair top kicker. Raising risks bloating pot OOP with a hand that doesn't want to go broke light; folding is too tight given odds and hand strength. Plan: call and play postflop aggressively if we hit.

I was the preflop aggressor with A♣7♠ and the flop is 5♦-6♦-4♣. This board is somewhat connected and two diamonds are out, but I still have decent equity: three Aces plus eight straights (3s or 8s) = ~11 outs to improve. Against Elon's calling range (lots of broadways, small pairs and suited connectors), a continuation-bet here gives fold equity and charges the drawing hands. I’ll size to ~50% pot to balance denial of equity and keeping worse hands and draws paying to see a turn. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I can proceed depending on the turn (value-bet A/7/3/8, pot-control on blanks, fold to big flop raises which usually represent sets/straights).

Flop board 5d6d4c is extremely draw-heavy with possible straights (23,37), flush draws (diamonds), and pairs. A8s has no pair, no straight draw (78 needed for OESD), no flush draw (spades not on board, just backdoor). Pure ace-high with ~6 outs to top pair/top kicker, but vulnerable to Minni's aggressive SB range which includes overpairs, sets, two-pair, combo draws like 3d7d/2d7d/7x suited, or bluffs. Her 1/3 pot c-bet is standard continuation vs called range. OOP with no equity and low SPR (Minni ~3900 behind), calling commits too much to a bluff-catcher without initiative. Raising as a bluff is poor OOP on wet board vs aggressor who may shove draws. Preflop call was fine for speculative suited ace, but no hit means fold to avoid tough turn spots. History shows Minni cbets light but board favors her value range more. Pot odds 3:1 but poor implied odds going forward.

3♣9♥ is a very weak, unsuited, and poorly connected hand. Facing 100 to call into a 150 pot, the hand has low equity versus the wide defending ranges of the SB and BB and is frequently dominated (any 9 or 3 with a better kicker or any ace/face will dominate). Raising as a steal is an option on the button, but both opponents are deep-stacked and likely to defend or 3-bet; I don’t want to open this marginal hand and get into a difficult postflop or 3‑bet pot. Preserving my 5,100 stack and waiting for a more playable spot is the correct play here.

5d7h is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Facing the big blind with a deep stack of 25k+, completing the SB gives poor implied odds and post-flop disadvantage OOP against a wide defending range. Folding saves chips for better spots, prioritizing stack preservation in a long 150-hand game.

Folded to me on the button preflop in a 3-handed pot. 2d6c is a weak hand but well within the profitable stealing range from the button against a single big blind due to position and fold equity. Minni in BB has 50BB effective stack and from hand history often defends loosely but folds to aggression frequently (e.g., many checks and folds postflop). Raising to 3BB (300 total) is standard size for button steals: high fold equity wins the 150 dead money immediately ~70% of time, and if called, ~35-40% equity vs her calling range plus position and implied odds. Limping is poor OOP postflop, folding too tight here leaks EV long-term over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s4s, which is a very weak hand even for its suitedness. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds are okay, playing out of position with a hand that is easily dominated and has poor post-flop playability is generally not profitable. Given the aggressive nature of the table and the fact that I am facing a raise from the Button with a player still behind me in the Big Blind, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Elon opened to 300 from the button. I have A♠T♦ in the big blind with a 5,000 (≈50bb) stack. ATo is strong enough to continue heads‑up, but out of position and vs a wide/aggressive opener I prefer to keep the pot manageable rather than 3‑bet to 500 (minimal 3‑bet gives little fold equity and can get me OOP into bigger pots or a 4‑bet). Pot odds are attractive to defend (200 more to call into a 450 pot — break‑even ≈31%), and ATo has reasonable equity vs a typical button opening range. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — play aggressively if I hit (top pair, good draw), otherwise use pot control and fold to huge pressure on very unfavorable boards.

Check. I missed the flop — A♠T♦ on K♥Q♥6♦ has only Ace-high plus a one-card Broadway straight draw (J = 4 outs) and 3 remaining Aces (total ~7 nominal outs, ~28% to improve by river). I’m out of position against a wide/aggressive button opener who will c-bet a lot; with ~48bb effective and a pot of 650 there’s no reason to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range, and lets me call a small c-bet to realize equity. Plan: check; if Elon makes a small c-bet (~1/3 pot) I will call and reevaluate on turn; fold to very large pressure/raises unless the turn brings a clear improvement. This preserves stack while extracting value when I hit.

Flop 6d Kh Qh gives me bottom pair (6s) with a terrible 2 kicker. Board is draw-heavy with KQ broadway and heart flush draw possible. Minni called preflop OOP with a wide range (pairs, broadway, suited connectors, Ax suited) but history shows she often checks and folds to cbets when missing (e.g., Hand 28, 40, 37 where similar dynamics led to folds). As PFR with position, my range hits K/Q/A hard and includes bluffs, so cbetting rep is strong. Thin value/protection bet to charge draws (hearts have ~35% equity), deny equity, and take down missed hands frequently (~60-70% vs her range). Small size 350 (~half pot) maximizes fold equity while keeping pot small OOP for her calls/shoves. Equity ~35-40% vs her range, plus fold equity + position = +EV. Check-back risks giving free card to overcards/draws that beat me. Her 48BB stack, unlikely to hero vs small bet.

Elon (button) c-bet 350 into a 1000 pot (~35% pot). I have A♠T♦ on K♥Q♥6♦ — I’ve missed but have 7 clean outs (3 Aces + 4 Jacks) giving ~31% to improve by the river. Break-even equity to call is 350/(1000+350) ≈ 25.9%, so a call is +EV. Out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin 3‑bet; calling keeps his bluffs and worse hands in, realizes my equity, and preserves stack. Plan: call the 350, reevaluate on the turn — fold to large turn/river pressure unless I pair the Ace or make the straight; raise/lead only if I improve and get value.

Call 350. Elon’s turn bet is small (~35% pot) and I’m out of position with A♠T♦ on K♥Q♥6♦3♦ — I’ve missed but have meaningful outs (3 Aces + 4 Jacks). Raising OOP with just Ace‑high is too thin; calling realizes equity, keeps his bluffs and worse hands in the pot, and preserves my stack (I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP). Plan: call to see the river; if I pair the Ace or make the straight I’ll look for value/raise, otherwise fold to large river aggression.

Turn board 6d Kh Qh 3d pairs my 6c for bottom pair weak kicker, with backdoor diamond (2d). Minni checked OOP again after calling flop cbet on draw-heavy board. Her range: wide BB defense (Kx, Qx, Ax suited, suited connectors, pairs) that includes value, draws (hearts preflop, diamonds now completed for some). 3d is somewhat blank but strengthens diamond draws, weakens my relative hand strength. However, as PFR with position, my range (KQ, KJ, ATs+, pocket pairs) is perceived strong, hits top often. History shows Minni checks/folds to continued aggression OOP frequently (e.g., Hands 28,37,40,41). Pot control checks risk giving free river to draws/overcards that beat me (~40% equity vs her range drops if they improve). Thin value/protection bet ~1/2 pot extracts from worse pairs (77-99, missed draws like heart draws now semi-bluff folding), denies equity, high fold equity (~60%+ take it down). Her 4450 stack (~3.3x pot) unlikely to shove wide vs small bet. Position allows controlling pot OOP for her. Bet 700 builds pot profitably with ~35% equity when called + fold equity + position EV. Check risks checked back showdown loss or free card.

Re-evaluating after the turn bet: I have A♠T♦ on 6♦ K♥ Q♥ 3♦ and Elon bets 700 into a 2050 pot. With only the river to come my clean outs are 3 Aces + 4 Jacks = 7 outs, so equity ≈ 7/46 ≈ 15.2%. The call requires ~700/(2050+700) ≈ 25.5% equity to break even. My equity is well below the pot‑odds threshold, and I’m out of position against a preflop BTN opener who will have a lot of Kx/Qx value hands that I’m currently behind to. Raising here would be a thin bluff that I don’t expect to fold enough of his value range and would commit too many chips with little real equity. Previously I planned to call small turn bets, but at this sizing the correct play is to conserve chips and fold.

I have a strong starting hand (AQo) on the button in a three-handed game. This is a standard raising opportunity to build the pot and take initiative. My stack is significantly larger than my opponents', allowing me to play aggressively. I'll open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Pocket fives in the small blind vs a button open to 300 with stacks ~44bb (me) / 99bb (BB) / 252bb (BTN). This is a classic set‑mining situation — calling keeps the pot manageable and gives good implied odds to hit a set. A min 3‑bet to 500 is too small to credibly take it down and will often be called, leaving me out of position in a bigger pot. Folding is too nitty with a medium pair and these stacks. I’ll call the raise and see a flop; if the BB 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing, and if I see a favorable flop I’ll look to extract value with sets or fold vs heavy aggression with only overcards.

Facing a button raise from Flash (aggressive player based on history) and a call from Minni in the small blind, with Ac6h offsuit in the big blind out of position. A6o has decent blocker value but weak kicker and no suited/connectors potential. Pot odds are 3.5:1 (200 to call into 700), but multiway pot OOP postflop is tricky with vulnerable top pair potential. Ranges: Flash's button raise is wide, Minni's call tighter. Equity realization poor multiway. Folding preserves stack for better spots; no strong reason to defend this marginal ace.

Check. Pocket fives are an overpair to this low, mostly dry 3♣-4♣-2♥ board but I’m out of position vs the button’s wide opening range. Checking controls the pot, lets Flash barrel with a wide range (which I can call for value), and avoids bloating the pot when I’m OOP and vulnerable to overpairs/sets. If Flash c-bets a standard size I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn; if he checks back I’ll take a free card and often bet a safe size on a harmless turn. I’ll be prepared to fold to very large raises that show clear strength or to remove myself if I miss future streets.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on a low, connected board (3c-4c-2h) after raising pre-flop. I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). My opponent in the Small Blind checked to me. As the pre-flop aggressor, this is a good spot to continuation bet. A bet of 350 (half the pot) allows me to take the initiative, potentially fold out better hands or random K-high/under-pair hands, and I still have equity to improve on later streets.

Call. Pocket fives are an overpair to this dry 3♣-4♣-2♥ flop and Flash (button) opened a wide range, so a standard 350 c-bet will often be a continuation with many hands I beat (broadways, missed suited connectors, weak one-pair hands). Calling controls the pot out of position, keeps worse hands and floats in the pot, and gives me correct pot odds (calling 350 to win ~1400 requires only ~20% equity). A raise here bloats the pot OOP and gets me into uncomfortable spots vs sets or a shove from a deep-stacked opponent. Plan: call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn — proceed for value on harmless cards, fold to a very large raise or heavy polarizing action that shows set/two‑pair strength.

Check. The A♠ on the turn is a very dangerous overcard that sits squarely in Flash’s open-raising range. I’m out of position with a vulnerable medium pair (5♣5♥) so I want to control the pot size and keep his bluffs in — checking achieves that. If Flash now fires a small-to-medium bet (roughly up to ~1/3–1/2 pot, ~450–700) I’ll give a call to deny him free cards and extract from worse one-pair hands; if he jams or puts in a big polarizing/pot-sized bet I’ll fold because my hand has poor fold equity and little showdown value vs two‑pair/sets. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river and look to get thin value on safe runouts or check behind against aggression.

The turn brings the Ace of Spades, giving me top pair with a Queen kicker. The board is quite wet with 3c-4c-2h, meaning any 5 makes a straight. Since Minni checked again, I will continue to bet for value and protection against draws. An Ace is very much in my range as the pre-flop raiser, and while I need to be cautious of a 5, I currently have a strong hand against most of Minni's calling range. I will bet 700, which is half the pot, to keep building the pot and price out potential draws.

Call 700. I’m OOP with pocket 5s and the A♠ is a worrying overcard that hits a chunk of Flash’s button opening range (many Ax, and A3/A4/A2/A5 specifically make two‑pair vs me). However Flash’s line (raised pre, 350 c‑bet on flop, 700 into ~1400 on turn — ~1/2 pot) is exactly the kind of wide, medium‑sized betting I’d expect with a mix of value and lots of missed/weak hands (broadways, Kx, club draws, underpairs). Folding is too nitty given the odds; raising OOP bloats the pot and puts me in tough spots vs sets/two‑pair or a shove. Calling 700 gets me ~25% break‑even equity (700/(2100+700) ≈ 25%), which is affordable given how often I still beat his bluffs and some single‑pair holdings. Plan: call and see the river; fold to a large raise/shove (polarized range), call or small‑bet for thin value on safe rivers, and be cautious if a club or a paired/risky river completes obvious stronger hands.

Check. The river (Td) brings more hands that beat my pocket 5s — any T, any A (and A2/A3/A4 two‑pair combos), and sets are all in Flash’s plausible line.
